Title: Voltammetric sensing of heavy metals for global water security: a critical materials perspective on field-deployable monitoring platforms
Description:
Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain
            the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in
            Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles
            and JavaScript.

Advertisement


npj Clean Water
volume 9,  Article number: 48 (2026) Cite this article


Heavy metal contamination necessitates portable, sensitive voltammetric sensors. This review assesses electrode interface engineering using MOFs, COFs, and carbon nanocomposites, connecting structural attributes (porosity, redox sites, conductivity) to sensitivity, selectivity, and limit of detection. Integration with microfluidics and edge-deployable machine learning enables autonomous monitoring, yet challenges matrix interference, fouling, model transferability, and standards lack persistence. A research roadmap for robust, field-deployable sensors for decentralized water quality surveillance is outlined.

Heavy metal contamination in aquatic systems poses a significant and growing environmental challenge globally, threatening both ecosystem integrity and public health. Unlike organic pollutants, heavy metals are non-biodegradable and can persist in the environment. Consequently, these persistent contaminants accumulate in various environmental compartments, including sediments, the water column, and within living organisms through processes of bioaccumulation and biomagnification across trophic levels. The enduring presence and toxicity of heavy metals in water sources are serious concerns for environmental monitoring initiatives1,2.

Anthropogenic sources are the main contributors to heavy metals contamination, primarily responsible for the contemporary influx of heavy metals into aquatic environments. Industrial discharge is the leading contributor, releasing toxic metals like Lead (Pb), Copper (Cu), Arsenic (As), Cadmium (Cd), Chromium (Cr), Nickel (Ni), and Mercury (Hg), from processes such as mining, Coal combustion, smelting, metal plating, and chemical manufacturing3,4. Urban and municipal runoff also contributes heavy metals from sources such as vehicle emissions, sewage, landfill leachates, and aging infrastructure. Furthermore, agricultural practices introduce metals such as Cd and As through phosphate fertilizers and pesticides, which can subsequently contaminate groundwater5,6. Natural processes establish a background level of contamination, primarily through the weathering of metal-containing rocks and volcanic eruptions7.

Even at trace concentrations, heavy metals are harmful because of their reactivity, mainly disrupting cellular function by binding to essential biological molecules such as enzymes and proteins. This interference frequently triggers the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which induce oxidative stress and subsequent cellular damage8. A critical concern is the strong potential for these metals to bioaccumulate and biomagnify within the aquatic food chain, ultimately leading to human exposure. This exposure, particularly through contaminated drinking water and seafood consumption, is significantly correlated with severe health consequences9. Adverse health impacts span a wide spectrum, including kidney dysfunction, neurological disorders, cardiovascular issues, and multiple types of cancer8,9. The World Health Organization (WHO) highlights that environmental determinants, particularly water contamination, substantially contribute to the global disease burden10. A detailed synopsis of the predominant hazardous heavy metals, their associated activities, and health implications is summarized in Table 1 and Figs. 1 and 2.

Schematic illustration of heavy metal pollution sources (anthropogenic and natural), their release into the hydrosphere, lithosphere, and atmosphere, and the main human exposure routes (food chain/oral ingestion, inhalation, and dermal contact). This Figure was created with BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://BioRender.com/leumi3l).

The schematic illustrates the pathway from free radical overproduction and oxidative stress to systemic organ-level damage, including neurotoxicity, genotoxicity, and cardiovascular dysfunction. This Figure was created with BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://BioRender.com/mk0ejjg).

In response to the serious public health risks posed by heavy metal contamination, various international and national regulatory agencies have established enforceable drinking water quality standards11. These guidelines are fundamental for the protection of public water supplies and for informing remediation Actions. Among these, the Guidelines for drinking-water quality established by WHO serve as a scientific foundation for countries worldwide to formulate their national standards12. The Environmental Protection Agency in the United States (US EPA) is tasked with defining Maximum Contaminant Levels (MCLs) under the Safe Drinking Water Act. These MCLs are legally enforced standards that pertain to public water systems, set based on assessments of health risks, technological feasibility, and cost-benefit evaluations. Similarly, the European Union has enacted stringent standards under the Drinking Water Directive (EU DWD), which specifies parametric values for a range of contaminants, including heavy metals, with which all member countries must comply12 The MCLs for various heavy metals, as specified by the WHO, EU DWD, and US EPA, are presented in Table 1.

Currently, gold standard analytical techniques for detecting trace heavy metals in water are advanced spectroscopic techniques, particularly atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS)9. These techniques provide superior analytical performance, especially regarding sensitivity and simultaneous multi-element analysis. ICP-MS stands out by providing ultra-trace detection limits, often at parts-per-trillion levels, rendering it the benchmark for research and regulatory compliance13. While AAS remains a cost-effective alternative for analyzing single elements, ICP-OES is recognized for its high throughput and multi-element analysis capabilities14,15. Despite their effectiveness, these traditional techniques possess considerable limitations that restrict their usage, particularly in resource-limited situations and for on-site assessments16. Both ICP-OES and ICP-MS instruments require considerable initial investment, high operational costs (such as argon gas), costly consumables, and specialized laboratory setups17,18. Furthermore, the operation and maintenance of these systems require well-trained technical personnel, and the complex sample preparation steps are both time-consuming and vulnerable to contamination19,20. The large and delicate nature of these instruments renders them non-portable, necessitating that samples be collected and transported to a centralized laboratory, which can lead to delays and potential degradation16. Additionally, ICP techniques can be affected by spectral and matrix interferences, whereas AAS demands individual element lamps and specialized accessories for volatile metals such as mercury and arsenic. Moreover, these routine techniques usually report only total elemental concentrations, failing to differentiate oxidation states (e.g., Cr(VI) and Cr(III)), which is a key factor in toxicity evaluation9,21. These constraints highlight the demand for a portable, low-cost analytical system that combines selectivity, sensitivity, and the ability to deliver immediate results.

Modern voltammetric approaches are tailored to close this gap, with methods already utilized by the American Public Health Association (APHA method 3130B) for the detection of Zn, Cd, and Pb. Voltammetry is a dynamic electroanalytical technique that applies a time-dependent potential to a working electrode and measures the resulting current, which is proportional to the analyte concentration. This fundamental principle lies in examining the current–potential relationship to extract information about the analyte's electrochemical behavior at the electrode–electrolyte interface22. The distinct interplay between the applied potential and the resulting current generates an electrochemical profile that is characteristic of each analyte, providing valuable insights into its redox behavior, where the peak potential identifies the analyte (qualitative information), and the peak current quantifies its concentration (quantitative information)23. A voltammetric measurement typically involves a three-electrode configuration, consisting of a working electrode (W.E), a counter electrode (C.E), and a reference electrode (R.E)23 as illustrated in Fig. 3. The W.E facilitates the redox process and is typically fabricated from materials such as platinum, glassy carbon, carbon paste electrode, gold, or mercury, selected according to the analyte and potential range considerations24. Recent advancements have resulted in a widespread embrace of nanocomposites, including carbonous nanomaterial, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), Metal and Metal Oxide Nanoparticles (NPs), and conducting polymer hybrids, such as W.E modifiers25. These innovative materials leverage their unique electronic and structural properties to achieve improved selectivity and ultra-low detection limits, significantly outperforming traditional bare or basic film-coated electrodes.

A three-electrode cell include working electrode (either a carbon-paste electrode or a glassy carbon electrode), a platinum wire counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode in 3 M KCl. Fabrication of the working electrode by drop-casting of the sensing material onto the electrode surface or using a carbon-paste mixture. Performance note: Drop-casting is simple but suffers from batch-to-batch variability (RSD 5–15%), while carbon paste electrodes allow easy surface renewal. The working electrode material and fabrication method directly determine electroactive surface area, electron transfer kinetics, and overall sensitivity. This Figure was created using BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://biorender.com/iras372).

The C.E, also known as the auxiliary electrode, completes the circuit by balancing the electrical current flowing to or from the working electrode, facilitating the desired redox reactions while not interfering with the potential measurement fabricated from materials such as platinum, or graphite26. The R.E. provides a stable and known voltage, acting as a benchmark for evaluating the potential of the W.E in an electrochemical cell, enabling accurate, drift-free measurements27. The Ag/AgCl electrode is a commonly used reference electrode type, which is appreciated for its stability and broad applicability. It typically consists of a silver wire coated with silver chloride AgCl immersed in KCl solution23, while Hg/Hg2Cl2 (Calomel) is another traditional option recognized for stability but less frequently utilized due to mercury issues27. This voltametric technique allows for the development of analytical systems that are not only highly sensitive but also simple, cost-effective, and portable.

Our unique contribution to the extant literature is multifaceted, encompassing a systematic categorization and rigorous critical assessment of the performance metrics—including selectivity, limit of detection, long-term stability, fouling resistance, and cost-effectiveness—of emerging electrode materials, including MOFs, COFs, and advanced carbon nanocomposites, thereby advancing beyond mere compilation to a profound evaluation of their suitability for real-world applications. This transcends a mere aggregation of data, providing a deep evaluation of their practical applicability. We further elucidate fundamental structure-property relationships, detailing how intrinsic material attributes such as porosity, surface functionality, redox activity, and electronic conductivity govern interfacial charge transfer kinetics, ion preconcentration, and signal amplification. The review also offers a comprehensive examination of the convergence of voltametric sensors with microfluidic architectures and edge-deployable machine learning (ML) models, critically analyzing the inherent trade-offs between predictive power and mechanistic interpretability. Furthermore, we identify and systematically analyze critical impediments to widespread adoption, including matrix interference, signal drift, electrode fouling, material scalability, and ML model transferability, as well as the lack of standardized protocols. The review concludes with a targeted research roadmap, guiding future efforts towards the development of robust, sustainable, and field-deployable voltametric sensors for decentralized water quality monitoring and intelligent pollution management. This review aims to be more than descriptive, offering a critical and forward-looking synthesis across materials chemistry, electrochemical engineering, and artificial intelligence, to catalyze advancements in next-generation voltametric sensors for global water security.

Voltammetry is an electroanalytical method that quantifies the current generated at a working electrode as a function of the applied potential. The principle fundamentally relies on the controlled application of a potential waveform to facilitate a redox reaction at the electrode-solution interface. The resulting current provides valuable quantitative and qualitative information regarding the analyte22. In the process of heavy metal detection, metal ions (Mn+) are typically reduced from their solution phase to an elemental state (M0) on the electrode surface during a deposition phase, followed by the reverse oxidation (stripping) phase28. Mass transport of analytes to the electrode surface is governed by three primary mechanisms: migration (movement driven by the electric field), convection (movement resulting from stirring), and diffusion (movement resulting from concentration differences)29. For Successful electrochemical detection, a rapid and efficient electron transfer process is critical, which is greatly influenced by the working electrode material, its surface area, and its interaction with the analyte. In voltammetry, the measured current responds proportionally to the concentration of electroactive analyte, making it highly sensitive for trace-level measurements.

The Selection of an appropriate voltammetric methodology is critical for enhancing both analytical selectivity and sensitivity during the experimental measurements. Technique selection is primarily determined by the targeted sensitivity threshold, analyte chemistry, and sample matrix complexity. Standard voltammetric techniques, including anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), square wave voltammetry (SWV), and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), which vary in their potential profiles as shown in Fig. 4, and are broadly applied in high-sensitivity electroanalytical research [30]. For example, for detecting trace levels of heavy metals, approaches integrating a preconcentration step, most notably stripping voltammetry, are commonly favored due to their ability to significantly improve the signal-to-noise ratio.

Applied potential (E) vs time (t) waveforms for common voltammetric techniques. LSV linear ramp; high background current, limited to mechanistic studies. Cyclic voltammetry (CV): triangular waveform; mechanistic characterization only. ASV: Two-step technique of preconcentration (constant negative potential, 60–300 s) followed by stripping (anodic scan from –1.0 V to +0.2 V using LSV, DPV, or SWV). DPV: staircase ramp with superimposed pulses; current sampled before/after each pulse to suppress background. SWV: Staircase with symmetric forward/reverse pulses; fastest scan rates, highest sensitivity. LSV is a fundamental voltammetric technique in which a linear potential ramp is applied from an initial to a final potential while the resulting current is continuously recorded. It is particularly valuable for elucidating electrode mechanisms (e.g., probing electron-transfer kinetics) and serves as the reference waveform for stripping analyses, where a linear potential sweep is applied during the stripping step, typically without pulsed features. However, LSV is rarely selected for trace heavy metal quantification because the large background charging current results in comparatively high detection limits, typically above 100 µg/L. Consequently, for routine analytical applications that require sensitivity below 10 µg/L, DPV or SWV are generally preferred30,31.

Even though CV is typically hindered for ultra-trace measurement of heavy metals owing to considerable non-Faradaic capacitive charging currents, it remains essential for elucidating mechanistic details of the electrode/electrolyte interface32,33. This method involves linear ramping of the potential of the working electrode in a triangular waveform between defined switching limits, including the analyte to undergo forward reduction and reverse oxidation processes, while the resultant current generates a diagnostic hysteresis loop. Within the realm of heavy metal sensor development, CV is utilized as a primary diagnostic method to investigate the redox behavior of target ions (for example, the reversibility of the \({M}^{n+}/{M}^{0}\)) and to precisely define the electrochemical potential range necessary for subsequent stripping steps. However, CV is not suitable for trace metal quantification in environmental samples due to poor signal-to-noise ratio at low concentrations (typically > 100 µg/L). Use CV only for diagnostic and optimization purposes; switch to stripping voltammetry or pulsed techniques for actual sample analysis. Additionally, CV is fundamental in characterizing the kinetic behavior of modified electrodes, as assessing and analyzing the relationship between peak current and scan rate facilitates the differentiation between diffusion-controlled processes and surface-confined sorption processes34,35. Such characterization is crucial for validating the enhancement of both electron transfer kinetics and active surface area attributed to advanced nanocomposite modifiers.

ASV is the method of choice when the analytical goal is ultra-trace quantification (sub-µg/L to low µg/L) of metals that can be reductively deposited as amalgams or metallic films—notably Pb (II), Cd (II), Cu (II), Zn (II), and Hg (II). It is ideal for drinking water compliance monitoring, natural water surveys, and any application where detection limits below 10 µg/L are required. However, ASV is not suitable for metals that do not form amalgams (e.g., Fe, Ni, Co, As) unless they are converted to a detectable form. Intermetallic interferences (e.g., Cu–Zn) can occur in complex mixtures; if such interferences are suspected, consider using pulsed stripping variants (DPASV or SWASV) with shorter deposition times or chemical masking31,36. The procedure consists of two key steps: (i) deposition (preconcentration): Mn+ are reduced and deposited onto the W.E at a constant negative potential for a specified duration, thereby concentrating the analyte. (ii) Stripping: a linear or pulsed scan of the potential toward the positive (anodic) direction, prompting the re-oxidization of the deposited metal, which is then stripped back into the solution31. Hg, Bi, Au film electrodes are widely employed in ASV for the determination of trace metal species in natural waters, with frequent application to seawater samples37. For instance, voltammetric in situ profiling systems for online trace metal measurement employ a mercury-deposited iridium microelectrode that is further shielded by a gel layer38. An Au-film-modified glassy carbon working electrode was used for copper speciation in seawater via ASV. This approach quantified electroactive, acid-dissolved, inert, and totally dissolved copper, yielding results comparable to those from ICP-MS39. For a metal to be measurable by ASV, it must be reducible, capable of accumulating at the electrode surface, and subsequently re-oxidizable under defined potential conditions. Therefore, the technique is not universally suitable for all trace metals, and elements such as Fe, Ni, and Co typically require other voltammetric techniques for accurate quantification30.

CSV is employed for the determination of analytes that, during the preconcentration step, form insoluble salt or strongly adsorbed complexes with the electrode surface or species in solution. In contrast to ASV, preconcentration occurs at positive potential through anodic film formation or adsorption of a surface layer, whereas the stripping phase necessitates a negative scan in which the deposited species is cathodically reduced and stripped from the electrode30. This approach is particularly suited for the analysis of species including sulfides, halides, and selected Mn+, including As (III) and, under appropriate conditions, Hg (II), often in the form of surface complexes or sparingly soluble salts40. For example, gold wire was used as a W.E for As(III) detection by CSV on various samples of groundwater, achieving a LOD (3 × standard deviation of multiple successive determinations (σ) method) of 0.037 μg/L examined in simulated deionized water with real samples validated against ICP-MS; however, the analytical response was adversely affected by the presence of chloride and humic acid41. In addition, CSV is the preferred technique when the analyte does not form a reversible amalgam but can be oxidatively deposited as an insoluble salt or a strong complex on the electrode surface. Typical targets include As (III), Se (IV), halides, and certain sulfur-containing species. CSV is also uniquely powerful for speciation, distinguishing between oxidation states such as As(III)/As(V) or Cr(III)/Cr(VI) by carefully selecting the deposition potential or using selective complexing ligands. Use CSV when speciation is critical for toxicity assessment, but be aware that organic matter and chloride can strongly suppress signals; sample pretreatment or standard addition is often necessary41,42.

SWV and DPV, which are based on pulsed potential modes, achieve sensitivity levels superior to those of linear scan techniques, through the effective suppression of the non-faradaic charging current43. In both approaches, the applied potential takes the form of a staircase ramp onto which a series of small potential pulses is superimposed43. The current is determined immediately prior to the application of each pulse and again at the end of the pulse, after which the difference ΔI is graphed relative to the base potential. In practice, SWV is generally chosen due to its higher sensitivity and faster scan rates, as it records both forward and reverse currents during each pulse, thereby enhancing the faradaic component44. These approaches are frequently combined with a subsequent stripping step (e.g., SWASV) to achieve ultra-trace detection thresholds. For instance, a nanocomposite of SnO₂ combined with reduced graphene oxide (GO) has been used for the quantification of Pb(II), Cd(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), offering very low detection limits. In deionized spiked water, the detection limit evaluated by the 3σ method does not exceed 5.60 × 10−2 μg/L for these metal ions45. Both techniques are excellent for simultaneous multi-metal detection and for samples with overlapping stripping peaks. Choose DPV when peak resolution is the highest priority (e.g., Pb2⁺ and Sn2⁺ at low concentrations). Choose SWV when speed and sensitivity are more important—SWV scans up to 100 Hz, completing an analysis in seconds rather than minutes, and it is less affected by dissolved oxygen, making it ideal for field-deployable sensors. For ultra-trace detection, combine either technique with a stripping step (SWASV or DPASV). In complex matrices rich in organic foulants, use short deposition times (30–60 s) with SWV to minimize electrode fouling31,46,47.

The overall analytical response of a voltammetric sensor is critically influenced by a combination of operational conditions and material-related characteristics of the sensing interface. Effective tuning of these parameters is fundamental to maximizing signal-to-noise ratio, controlling matrix-related interferences, and attaining the lowest practicable detection limits for the heavy metal analytes. Each of the key parameters governing voltammetric performance can be described in detail, and their respective roles can be outlined for applications in trace heavy metal analysis.

The deposition potential corresponds to the fixed potential used during the preconcentration stage; it must be adequately negative in ASV to drive efficient electroreduction and immobilization of the metal ions on the electrode surface48. For example, nano-cellulose modified electrodes were used for the detection of Cd(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), where an optimum deposition potential of −0.90 V was selected to ensure efficient preconcentration of all metal ions48. The deposition time, which specifies how long preconcentration is maintained, is directly proportional to the analyte loading on the electrode and therefore improves sensitivity with reduced detection limits; although excessive deposition periods lengthen the analysis and can induce electrode fouling49. The scan rate represents the rate of potential change in the stripping phase and influences both peak height and resolution, as faster scans usually yield larger peak currents yet tend to broaden peaks and diminish the separation between closely spaced features50. For example, in this study a mercury working electrode was employed for the simultaneous determination of Tl(I), Pb(II) and Cu(II), and it was shown that increasing the scan rate in DPV enhanced the peak current but led to peak broadening and poorer separation of the metal signals; thus an intermediate scan rate of 10 mV/s was chosen to balance sensitivity and resolution50. The pulse amplitude, which denotes the height of the potential steps in DPV or SWV, boosts faradaic current and analytical sensitivity as it is raised, but at the cost of a higher background signal and potential peak shape distortion51. Finally, the electrode material, including both its surface morphology and chemical composition, plays a central role in governing selectivity, controlling overpotential, and facilitating efficient preconcentration through adsorption or complexation, thereby strongly affecting the sensor's sensitivity and robustness52,53.

Traditional electrode materials have historically underpinned electrochemical strategies for the detection and removal of heavy metals in water treatment applications. Within analytical chemistry, their use has been especially prominent in stripping voltammetry, where their characteristic physicochemical behavior facilitates highly sensitive and selective measurement of toxic metal ions. Although recent research increasingly prioritizes alternative electrode materials in response to toxicity and cost issues, a clear understanding of the performance and constraints of these conventional systems remains essential for interpreting contemporary advances. Mercury-based electrodes have been widely valued for their elevated hydrogen overpotential and their renewable, smooth surface, attributes that render them especially well-suited to ASV54. Mercury-based electrodes, including the hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE) and the dropping mercury electrode (DME), have long been recognized for their outstanding analytical performance. Their capacity to form amalgams with a wide range of metal ions enables preconcentration of the analyte before the stripping stage, thereby achieving very low detection limits. Historically, the bubbling mercury electrode illustrated this advantage by enabling the determination of zinc, cadmium, lead, and copper in natural and wastewater samples, where a clear proportionality between analyte concentration and peak current was established54. Additionally, mercury electrodes face several limitations, including contamination in complex sample matrices, time-consuming cleaning procedures, and poor long-term stability. Consequently, research into sustainable alternatives has accelerated. Mercury-free materials aim to match or exceed the analytical performance of mercury-based electrodes while reducing both environmental and health hazards55. Despite the advantageous physicochemical features of mercury and its broad use in trace-level heavy metal analysis, both the dropping mercury and HMDEs exhibit insufficient mechanical stability for automated applications, since their droplets can detach easily. The substantial consumption of elemental mercury in these systems also contradicts current efforts toward sustainable analytical chemistry, where carbon-based and surface-modified materials are favored to improve analytical sensitivity56,57.

A study focusing on a rice agroecosystem employed HMDE for the simultaneous determination of trace metals and reported highly favorable analytical figures of merit. The simultaneous determination of Zn(II), Cd(II), and Pb(II) was performed with a HMDE in 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.5). For each metal, the anodic stripping peak heights varied linearly over the concentration range 20-100 µg/L. The limits of detection were 4 µg/L for Cd, 10 µg/L for Pb, and 10 µg/L for Zn. Collectively, these data demonstrate the electrode's robustness for multi-element analysis at trace levels pertinent to water quality assessment58. One reported approach utilized a thin-film mercury electrode (TFME) formed on carbon tips to simultaneously measure Cd(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II). This electrode architecture not only enabled multi-metal analysis but also improved sensitivity and achieved lower limits of detection compared to unmodified electrodes, while the experimental results were accurately captured by an artificial neural network model \(\left({R}^{2},|,0.9933\right)\)59. One critical investigation of mercury-film-modified commercial screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) for Pb(II) sensing reported a satisfactory LOD of 8.9 µg/L in ultrapure spiked water using ASV, but also highlighted problems associated with non-uniform mercury deposition on the rough electrode substrate60.

Furthermore, the use of TFMEs, produced by electrodeposition on solid supports such as glassy carbon, represents one method for trace metal analysis. A study utilized low-current stripping chronopotentiometry (SCP) to quantify Fe(III) in filtered coastal and estuarine waters61. Despite its high sensitivity, the approach required multiple pre-treatment procedures, notably filtration and complexation with solochrom violet. The same electrochemical setup was also effective for detecting Sb(III) at ultra-trace levels in ultrapure simulated water, achieving a detection limit of 0.008 µg/L (3σ method), and was successfully applied to seawater samples using the standard addition method. Furthermore, the implementation of a double electrolysis potential during the preconcentration step effectively reduced interference from copper62. According to a study63, the mercury film electrode exhibited inadequate sensitivity and reproducibility when applied to Se(IV) analysis. The inclusion of Cu(II) ions in the plating process on the GC electrode effectively resolved this limitation. The dissolved metallic copper in the Hg film was identified as the key factor responsible for current peak intensification. The optimized method yielded a LOD of 0.06 µg/L in deionized water/0.1 M HNO3 (signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 2) and a relative standard deviation of 5.2% (n = 5) for 1 µM Se(IV) determined by CSV63. Another study developed a potentiometric stripping analysis technique enabling the concurrent quantification of Cu(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) in seawater samples64. In this method, a mercury film was electrodeposited directly onto a rotating glassy carbon electrode before each measurement by applying a potential of −1.1 V vs SCE for 10 min. Following the deposition, an electrolysis-stripping sequence was executed. The resulting metal concentrations were benchmarked against a mixed standard solution containing the same analytes. The method achieved detection limits (2σ method) of 0.044 µg/L for Cu(II), 0.003 µg/L for Pb(II), and 0.001 µg/L for Cd(II), all evaluated from a natural seawater sample, although the total analysis time was relatively long at about 75 min.

Despite their exceptional and renewable atomically smooth surface that provides strong selectivity, the performance profile of mercury electrodes collapses under the criteria of operational durability and translational feasibility. Their mechanical instability prevents automation, while time-consuming cleaning and matrix contamination further limit practical use; moreover, long-term stability and reproducibility are compromised by non-uniform film depositions on solid substrates55,56,62,65,66. Critically, their intrinsic toxicity necessitates stringent handling, storage, and disposal protocols, increasing operational cost and precluding field-deployable, single-use sensing formats. Collectively, these limitations have driven a decisive shift away from this class, driving a transition to solid-state electrodes.

Carbon electrodes represent a fundamental class of materials in electrochemical sensor technology for heavy metal detection, providing an advantageous combination of electrochemical efficiency, adaptability, and environmental sustainability. These electrodes are the most versatile and widely adopted solid-state platforms for heavy metal voltammetry, yet their analytical performance cannot be generalized; it is fundamentally dictated by the choice of carbon substrate, the strategy used to modify its interface, and the electrochemical protocol employed. Their remarkable characteristics-high electrical conductivity, substantial specific surface area, and modifiable surface chemistry-make them particularly effective for the sensitive and selective quantification of toxic metal ions such as Pb(II), Cd(II), and Hg(II) in environmental samples67. Typically, the detection process entails an initial enrichment of metal ions on the electrode surface driven by adsorption or electrostatic forces, succeeded by ASV measurement that generates distinct current signals correlated with analyte concentration68. This strategy overcomes key drawbacks of traditional spectroscopic methods, which, although highly accurate, depend on costly instruments, laborious sample pretreatment, and are generally unsuitable for in situ applications69. The raw analytical performance is attributable to the modifier, and advances in nanomaterial science have markedly improved the analytical capabilities of carbon-based electrodes, with graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and emerging nanocomposites being widely investigated as electrode modifiers70. For example, a biosensor based on GO composite onto screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) was reported for heavy metal detection. The LOD (Inhibitory Concentration (IC15)) for Hg2⁺, Pb2⁺, and Cd2⁺ were 0.0075 µg L−1, 0.015 µg L−1, and 0.0312 µg L−1, respectively, determined in buffered measurement solution71. Similarly, functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have enabled Pb(II) detection with a LOD of 2.1 µg/L in simulated double distilled water68. Furthermore, advanced sensor platforms that integrate artificial intelligence with carbon nanomaterial-based electrodes have demonstrated outstanding performance, enabling the concurrent determination of several metal ions with limits of detection of 1.6 µg/L for Pb(II) and 1.9 µg/L for Hg(II) using the 3σ method69.

SPCEs and carbon paste electrodes (CPEs) have attracted considerable interest due to their suitability for portable, field-deployable sensing platforms. Somerset and co-workers fabricated conducting polymer-coated SPCEs and modified CPEs for the electrochemical determination of Hg(II), Pb(II), Ni(II), and Cd(II). The SPCE modified with a polyaniline-poly(2,2'-dithiodianiline) (PANI-PDTDA) film exhibited an exceptionally low LOD of 4.41 × 10−5 µg/L in laboratory model solutions (calculated using the 3σ method), with linearity observed across the concentration range of 0.20–200 µg/L72. Sensitivity and LOD are thus governed not by the substrate itself, but by interfacial design and the reproducibility of the modification step.

Selectivity in carbon-based sensors is not intrinsic to the substrate but is conferred by the modifier's chemical functionality, typically through coordination-based analyte chelation or electrostatic exclusion of matrix interferents. For instance, a sulfur-doped graphitic carbon nitride with sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (QDs) nanocomposite modified SPCE demonstrated excellent sensitivity for Hg(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II). The sensor exhibited wide linear ranges of approximately 1.50–300 µg/L for Hg(II), 16–600 µg/L for Cu(II), and 26–1100 µg/L for Pb(II), with corresponding limits of detection of 0.56 µg/L, 6.0 µg/L, and 10.0 µg/L (S/N = 3), respectively, measured in spiked ultrapure water73. This performance is fundamentally governed by the modifier's chemistry: the sulfur-doped nanocomposite provides high-affinity coordination sites that promote preconcentration and selective stripping analysis, while the carbon QD expands the active surface area and accelerates charge transfer. Practical applicability was demonstrated through good recoveries (95.3–106.4%) in tap water and juice samples. It should be noted that the robustness of this selectivity in unspiked environmental matrices containing humic substances, colloidal particles, and high levels of competing ions was not investigated in the study, leaving the sensor's performance under such field-relevant conditions to be established in future work.

From another perspective, the use of a porous carbon-fiber microelectrode coated with gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) enabled the parallel quantification of Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II). The LOD were determined in spiked deionized water to be 127 µg/L, 294 µg/L, 61.4 µg/L, and 148 µg/L for Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), respectively, each within its own linear concentration range (1124–12364 µg/L for Cd(II), 2072–22792 µg/L for Pb(II), 636–6991 µg/L for Cu(II), and 2006–22066 µg/L for Hg(II)). No interference between the metals was observed, and the method was further validated for simultaneous determination in lake water samples using the standard addition approach74. This study shows that modest \({\rm{LOD}}\) values can be offset by real-sample validation using standard addition, which substantially strengthens confidence in sensor performance and should be benchmarked across material classes.

GCEs are extensively employed as a basic working electrode for electrochemical analysis of heavy metal ions in wastewater, attributed to their excellent conductivity, wide operational potential range, minimal background current, and robust chemical inertness75. In trace-level determinations, the electrode's performance is generally optimized by surface modification with carefully selected materials to boost sensitivity and selectivity, frequently employing nanomaterials like graphene, metallic nanoparticles, and polymeric phases, or constructing composite films that expand the effective surface area and furnish specific binding domains for the analytes of interest76. The versatility of the GCE enables a wide range of functionalization approaches, establishing it as a fundamental platform in designing sensors for the determination of pollutants such as lead, cadmium, mercury, and arsenic in complex environmental matrices77,78. They offer outstanding analytical performance across a broad spectrum of metal ions and can be readily modified; notably, very low LOD values are attainable when a GCE is employed.

For instance, one study employed a simple Bi-film coating on the electrode surface, enabling detection limits in double-distilled synthetic water (S/N ratio of 3 method) of approximately 1.07, 0.93, 0.65, and 0.94 µg/L for Zn, Cd, Pb, and Cu, respectively. The analysis of real river samples showed good agreement with AAS results, without compromising accuracy or repeatability79. In one reported Pb(II) determination, a GCE functionalized with a graphene/Nafion composite layer afforded a linear dynamic range from 4.1 to 5000 µg/L, with a detection limit of 1.1 µg/L (3σ method) determined in spiked deionized water using DPV80. The Nafion composites on a GCE use the cation-exchange properties of Nafion to impart anti-interference capabilities and sensitivity in spiked river water. Even higher sensitivity was obtained when GO was covalently immobilized on the GCE, providing linear calibration ranges for Pb(II) and Cd(II) from 2.1 µg/L down to 2.1 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L and from 1.1 µg/L down to 1.1 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L, respectively. The corresponding limits of detection were remarkably low, at 5.18 × 10⁻⁵ µg/L for Pb(II) and 3.15 × 10⁻⁵ µg/L for Cd(II), as determined in spiked ultrapure water81. Among the latest advances is a pyrolytic glassy carbon-graphene composite electrode integrated within a microfluidic system, which attains detection limits of 0.020 µg/L for Pb(II) and 0.100 µg/L for Cd(II) in simulated deionized water (S/N ratio method). This configuration highlights how sensor architecture can be finely optimized to achieve sub-parts-per-trillion sensitivity78. In the cited studies, stability is typically assessed over only a few consecutive cycles or days, with no quantitative data on signal decay over weeks of continuous immersion or repeated use in contaminated matrices. Similarly, the resistance to common fouling agents, such as humic and fulvic acids, is seldom quantified; where real-water validation exists, it often relies on standard addition methods that do not isolate fouling effects from other matrix interferences. This lack of data represents a significant research gap, as it prevents a meaningful comparison with the inherent anti-fouling advantage offered by disposable SPCEs. Furthermore, the scalability of GCE modification processes, often involving manual drop-casting or in-situ electrodeposition, raises concerns about batch-to-batch reproducibility at a manufacturing level, an aspect that must be standardized before GCE-based sensors can transition from laboratory prototypes to commercial products.

Here, the carbon sub-classes sharply diverge, defining a clear trade-off in field deployability. SPCEs are inherently mass-producible using thick-film and printing technologies, offering low-cost, disposable platforms ideal for on-site sensing, which eliminates cumulative electrode fouling and cross-contamination concerns. Conversely, GCEs, while offering superior baseline performance due to their robust chemical inertness, are typically non-disposable polishing-dependent electrodes. Their performance for trace metal detection is thus markedly dependent on antifouling resistance and long-term stability of the modifying film, a critical property that is rarely subjected to rigorous, quantitative evaluation in the cited literature, representing a significant research gap. The cost-effectiveness of GCE-based sensors is high per-electrode but low per-measurement if reusability is proven; SPCEs offer the opposite trade-off.

In electrochemical analysis of heavy metal ions in water, gold electrodes now represent a key enabling technology, distinguished by their superior conductivity, favorable biocompatibility, and reproducible electrochemical characteristics. These electrodes can be employed in forms varying from simple bare gold to elaborately nanostructured systems, with each design tailored to enhance trace-level detection. Due to the intrinsic interaction of gold with specific metals, particularly mercury and arsenic, highly sensitive direct measurements are possible, and the chemical stability of gold as a support material allows the construction of advanced, functionally diverse sensing interfaces82,83. Modifying gold electrodes with nanostructured materials, such as AuNPs or nanoclusters, leads to a substantial improvement in analytical capability. The increased surface-to-volume ratio and elevated electrocatalytic activity of these nanomaterials generate a higher abundance of active sites that facilitate metal ion accumulation and electron transfer83,84.

For example, a gold electrode functionalized with gold nanoclusters (GNPs-Au) has shown remarkable sensitivity for the concurrent determination of lead and cadmium. The sensor provides a broad linear calibration range of 1–250 µg/L and an ultra-low detection limit of 0.001 µg/L for both analytes, measured in spiked ultrapure water, underscoring the effectiveness of nanoscale surface modification. The results show only minor deviations from those obtained by AAS, indicating high accuracy and reliability84. Similarly, nanocomposite strategies employing electrodes modified with graphene QD and gold nanoparticles have demonstrated excellent performance toward other toxic metals, such as mercury, achieving detection limits (S/N ratio method) down to 0.004 µg/L83. In addition, researchers facile sensing platform by electrochemically co-depositing a gold nanofilm onto a screen-printed carbon electrode for the detection of trace mercury (II). The sensor, tuned through response surface methodology, demonstrated a linear response from 1 to 40 µg/L and, after optimization of chloride conditions, achieved a highly sensitive detection limit of 0.16 µg/L (3σ method) in simulated deionized water. It also showed satisfactory performance in real water samples, with results in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS85. A recent study described a nanoporous gold (NPG)-modified screen-printed carbon electrode for the concurrent determination of Pb(II) and Cu(II) using ASV. The device achieved linear response intervals of 1–100 µg/L for Pb(II) and 10–100 µg/L for Cu(II), with corresponding detection limits of 0.4 µg/L and 5.4 µg/L (S/N ratio of 3 method) in simulated deionized water, indicating its reliability for monitoring these metals in drinking water. Measurements in real water samples were in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS86. Enhancing the performance of gold-based electrochemical platforms, one study described an IoT-integrated sensor that employs gold nanoparticle-functionalized carbon thread electrodes for multiplexed detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II). Using DPV, the sensor achieved LOD (3σ method) of 111 µg/L, 128 µg/L, 87.6 µg/L, and 144 µg/L for Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), respectively, in simulated deionized water. The approach was validated by examining real lake water samples using the standard addition approach47. A sophisticated study introduced a microfluidic multichannel electrochemical cell engineered for the simultaneous monitoring of multiple metal ions. In this configuration, a bare gold electrode was used to directly sense Hg(II), achieving a detection limit of 0.9 µg/L (3σ method) in spiked ultrapure water, while a gold electrode modified with a thin mercury film was employed for Pb(II), attaining an LOD as low as 0.1 µg/L. Application of this device to industrial effluents and river water demonstrated excellent concordance with ICP-OES measurements87.

The primary weakness of gold electrodes is their cost and poor resistance to fouling in complex matrices. This high material cost drives two key design strategies to improve translational feasibility: (1) using gold as a thin-film surface modifier on a low-cost, disposable substrate like an SPCE to reduce the total precious metal usage; and (2) integration into highly controlled microfluidic systems to manage sample matrix complexity and extend sensor life. The scalability of nanostructured gold electrodes via controlled electrodeposition is feasible, but maintaining high batch-to-batch reproducibility of complex nanocomposite interfaces remains a significant challenge.

Bismuth-film and antimony-film electrodes represent a direct interface engineering strategy to replace mercury: a non-toxic metal film is plated in-situ or ex-situ onto a carbon substrate. Their performance is a direct consequence of mimicking mercury's ability to form alloys, but with distinct property trade-offs that dictate their optimal protocol selection.

Bismuth film electrodes represent a promising advancement in electrochemical methodologies, offering attributes such as simple fabrication, heightened sensitivity, distinct peak resolution, tolerance to dissolved oxygen in aqueous phases, limited toxicity, and operational capability under strong alkaline conditions88,89. The use of bismuth is considered a viable and environmentally friendly alternative to mercury, which has traditionally been utilized in ASV for detecting heavy metals such as Cu(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), and Zn(II). For example, research reported the fabrication of a Nafion/bismuth nanoparticle-modified screen-printed graphene electrode, which they applied to the anodic stripping voltammetric detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II)90. The sensor achieved LOD of 0.28 µg/L for Pb(II) and 40.34 µg/L for Cd(II), confirming its effectiveness for on-site monitoring of environmental samples such as treated wastewater and bay water. The results were also benchmarked against those obtained by AAS, demonstrating good agreement.

In a distinct methodological direction, a study reported the fabrication of a bismuth-modified porous graphene heterostructure for the ultrasensitive determination of Cd(II)91. By coupling the bicontinuous porous network of bismuth with the superior electrical conductivity of graphene, the resulting sensor delivered a linear range of 0.11–1.12 µg/L and an exceptionally low detection limit of 0.011 µg/L(S/N ratio of 3 method) for Cd(II) in synthetic ultrapure water, highlighting the efficacy of nanostructured designs91. A further notable trend is the use of biopolymers for constructing eco-friendly sensing platforms. In this context, research developed a screen-printed carbon electrode modified with a bismuth-chitosan nanocomposite for the concurrent quantification of several metal ions92. The presence of chitosan, a biocompatible polymer, significantly enhanced the analytical response. Under optimized conditions, the device achieved LODs (3σ method) of 0.1 µg/L for Zn(II), 0.1 µg/L for Cd(II), and 0.2 µg/L for Pb(II) in synthetic deionized water. Applied to real wastewater collected from a mining operation and benchmarked against ICP-MS, the method yielded LOD of 0.4 µg/L for Cd(II) and 0.3 µg/L for Pb(II). The sensor showed high precision, with relative standard deviations ranging from 1.3% to 5.6%92. Innovation in electrochemical monitoring now extends beyond the composition of the sensing material to include sophisticated electrode design and operational methodology. An anonymous researcher reported the use of a bismuth film-modified glassy carbon rotating disk electrode for concurrent detection of Pb(II) and Cu(II) in Danube River samples using the standard addition method. Optimization of the experimental conditions, particularly the incorporation of EDTA as a complexing ligand and fine-tuning of the rotation speed, enabled the attainment of competitive detection limits (σ method) in synthetic ultrapure water of approximately 4.29 µg/L for Pb(II) and 0.67 µg/L for Cu(II)93.

The introduction of antimony film-modified electrodes has marked an important step forward in electrochemical heavy metal monitoring in wastewater, serving as a greener alternative to traditional mercury-based systems. Owing to their capacity to operate under more acidic conditions and their reduced toxicological impact, antimony film electrodes (SbFEs) conform well to the ethos of green analytical chemistry94,95. Typically, these electrodes function by electroplating an antimony film, in situ or ex situ, onto a carbon-based substrate such as a glassy carbon electrode or a screen-printed carbon electrode. After modification, the electrode preconcentrates heavy metal ions via reduction, which are subsequently reoxidized during an ASV scan, giving rise to current intensities proportional to their concentration. The quality of the electroanalytical response is critically influenced by both the type of carbon substrate and the morphology of the antimony film, prompting sustained efforts to design new nanocomposite materials that provide superior sensitivity and long-term stability95,96.

A sequential injection-ASV approach employing an in-situ deposited antimony film on a GO-modified screen-printed carbon electrode (SbF-GO-SPCE) exhibited outstanding analytical performance for the concurrent quantification of several heavy metals. Linear calibration ranges of 0.1–1.5 µmol L−1 were established for Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II), with LOD (3σ method) of approximately 6.1 µg/L, 5.4 µg/L, 3.8 µg/L, and 13.2 µg/L in synthetic ultrapure water, respectively, thereby demonstrating the system's enhanced capability for multi-element detection97,98. The electrode was successfully applied to determine these metals in real wastewater samples collected from sewage, fertilizer effluents, and seawater, and the results were compared with those obtained by AAS. A particularly noteworthy approach involved fabricating a reduced GO-antimony nanoparticles (rGO-Sb NPs) nanocomposite on a glassy carbon substrate. This electrochemical sensor capitalized on the excellent charge-transport performance of rGO in combination with the high specific surface area and catalytic efficiency of Sb NPs. The method provided very low detection limits (3σ method) in synthetic purified water: 5.1 µg/L for Cd(II), 3.4 µg/L for Pb(II), 2.2 µg/L for Cu(II), and 0.96 µg/L for Hg(II), indicating a substantial gain in sensitivity, particularly for Pb(II) and Hg(II) determination96. An additional innovative strategy was described that involved the application of two-dimensional (2D) antimonene. A Sb-SPCE was effectively employed for the simultaneous voltammetric determination of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using DPV. The sensor exhibited a linear response for Pb(II) in the range 1.1–128.3 µg/L with LOD (3σ method) of 0.3 µg/L, and for Cd(II) in the range 9.1–132.7 µg/L with a detection limit of 2.7 µg/L in spiked purified water. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using antimony allotropes themselves as the electrode modifier for trace analysis in real matrices such as tap water, providing performance comparable to that of ICP-MS99.

This class presents a significant trade-off between analytical merit and operational stability. Their low toxicity, compatibility, and low cost with SPCEs grant them excellent field scalability, deployability, and cost-effectiveness90,92. However, their major analytical limitation is their long-term stability and batch reproducibility. The morphology of the electroplated film is critically dependent on deposition parameters (time, potential, solution composition) and the underlying substrate roughness. This leads to film leaching during stripping cycles and poor sensor-to-sensor reproducibility, a problem well-documented for mercury films that is inherited by these analogous systems.

Electroanalytical chemistry has experienced a notable shift regarding the detection of heavy metal ions (HMIs) in water, greatly accelerated by the introduction of advanced nanomaterials. These materials overcome the inherent drawbacks of traditional electrodes, including slow electron transfer rates, small active surface area, and a higher chance of surface fouling. Among the diverse classes of emerging materials, carbon-based nanomaterials, MOF, metal NPs, and conducting polymers have gained remarkable attention due to their outstanding physicochemical properties, including high electrical conductivity, high surface area, chemical stability, and facile surface modification. Figure 5 maps the classification and taxonomy of advanced nanomaterials engineered to functionalize electrochemical interfaces for trace heavy metal detection.

Schematic classification and key properties of advanced nanomaterials employed to modify electrochemical interfaces for heavy metal detection, including metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (trade-off: high cost for noble metals), carbonaceous materials, polymeric materials (trade-off: limited mechanical stability and possible film swelling), QD (trade-off: moderate sensitivity unless combined with metals), and MOFs/COFs; trade-off: poor intrinsic conductivity requiring conductive composites). Performance note: no single class optimizes all analytical figures of merit; hybrid composites (e.g., MOF/CNT, Bi/rGO, Au/QDs) better balance sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and cost for field-deployable sensors. Original image by authors.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, from 0D QD to 2D graphene sheets, form cutting-edge platforms for voltammetric sensing. Their principal function is to amplify the electrochemically active area and promote efficient electron exchange at the electrode–electrolyte interface, thereby markedly reduced LOD for hazardous metals such as Pb, Cd, Hg, As, and Cu100.

Graphene, composed of a single atomic layer of \(s{p}^{2}\) bonded carbon, and its derivatives rGO and GO, have emerged as a benchmark material in high-performance electrochemical sensing platforms. The exceptional carrier mobility coupled with extensive theoretical surface area (\(2600\,{{\rm{m}}}^{2}\,{{\rm{g}}}^{-1}\)) of graphene serves as an efficient substrate for the accumulation of metal ions throughout the preconcentration stage of stripping voltammetry101.

Recent research underscores the utility of rGO, that restored \(s{p}^{2}\) carbon lattice and promotes conductivity compared to GO. As a case in point, an rGO/Bi nanocomposite electrode enabled trace detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II) in tap water using SWV, affording LOD of 0.46 and 0.66 µg/L in spiked ultrapure water, respectively102. Similarly, the Bi nanoparticle/laser-induced graphene sensor exhibited nearly identical limits of detection (3σ method, 0.5 and 0.8 µg/L) in synthetic deionized water, underscoring that the combined effect of the high-surface-area rGO framework and the mercury-like properties of Bi promotes alloy formation and enhances peak current responses103,104. These two studies exemplify a crucial design principle: the rGO scaffold provides the conductive and high-area platform, but the selectivity and preconcentration efficiency are imparted by the Bi component, which mimics mercury via amalgam formation. The trade-off is that the long-term stability of such composite films under repeated stripping cycles is rarely reported; gradual Bi leaching and rGO restacking can degrade performance over time, a gap that must be addressed for continuous monitoring applications.

Furthermore, Ag-functionalized rGO enabled sensitive As(III) detection via DPV, achieving a 0.24 µg/L LOD in spiked purified water using the 3σ method, with performance that showed consistent reproducibility and reliability in real water samples analyzed by the standard addition method105. This real‑sample validation using standard addition strengthens confidence in matrix robustness, though long‑term fouling data in organic‑rich natural waters remain absent. The introduction of ruthenium into the GO framework enabled electrochemical sensing of As(III), Cd(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II) by DPV, with LODs in simulated purified water of 0.17, 0.31, 0.32, and 0.29 µg/L, respectively. This system achieved simultaneous determination of these heavy metals in river and tap water samples, with the results validated by AAS106. The Ru/GO sensor is notable for its multi-element capability and successful cross-validation with AAS, yet the operational stability over 60days showed a marked decline for mercury (retained 73.8%), highlighting that even high-performance graphene-based sensors suffer from gradual signal degradation for certain analytes, a critical consideration for field deployment where recalibration is impractical.

Another graphene/dimethylglyoxime/Nafion-modified GCE was employed for the selective determination of Ni(II) ions in water samples via adsorptive stripping voltammetry in the presence of common interfering ions such as Co(II) and Zn(II). It demonstrated good selectivity, achieving a LOD (3σ method) of 1.5 µg/L in spiked ultrapure distilled water, which complies with drinking-water guideline levels107. Selectivity here is achieved via a dual mechanism: dimethylglyoxime provides specific Ni(II) chelation while the Nafion film electrostatically excludes anionic interferents-an elegant example of interfacial engineering that requires precise control over film thickness to maintain reproducibility across electrodes. A QDs/GO composite integrated on a GCE functions as an ASV platform capable of detecting Cd(II) and Pb(II) simultaneously, exploiting abundant oxygen groups and a large surface area to facilitate electrostatic accumulation of the metal species. The homogeneous dispersion of QDs enhances the metal re-oxidation kinetics during the stripping step, resulting in improved analytical performance compared with QDs/GCE and GO/GCE electrodes. This is evidenced by wide detection ranges and limits of detection (3σ method) for Cd(II) of 11.24–11241 μg/L with an LOD of 7.45 μg/L, and for Pb(II) of 20.72–10360 μg/L with an LOD of 1.17 μg/L, along with consistent recoveries in real water samples determined using the standard addition method108. Combining \(0\)D QD with \(2\)D GO can enhance surface area, conductivity, and binding-site density. However, the scalability of such composites remains constrained by the need for precise control over QD size, interfacial coupling, and loading uniformity.

Overall, graphene-based electrodes can achieve among the lowest reported LODs, and their selectivity is readily tuned through functionalization. Yet, literature is still dominated by short-term measurements in spiked samples. Quantitative assessments of fouling resistance in humic-acid-rich matrices, long-term (beyond several months) signal stability, and batch-to-batch reproducibility of drop-cast graphene films remain scarce.

MWCNTs, and single-walled (SWCNTs) are known for their high aspect ratio, electrical conductivity, surface area, excellent electron transfer, high transduction capacity, and remarkable mechanical strength. In the context of voltammetry, CNTs serve as “molecular wires”, facilitating electron transfer between redox species and the electrode surface109. An illustrative example is the modification of a GCE with an amino clay-based polymer, featuring MWCNTs to boost conductivity for the detection of Cu(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II) via the DPV technique. With the MWCNT network, rapid signal transduction is achieved, enabling excellent sensitivity in complex matrices. Under these conditions, LOD (S/N ratio of 3 method) of 1.0 × 10⁻⁴, 6.0 × 10⁻⁵, and 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L were obtained, with corresponding linear ranges of 3.18 × 10⁻⁴–6.36 × 101 µg/L, 2.01 × 10⁻⁴–2.01 × 102 µg/L, and 4.14 × 10⁻⁴–2.07 × 102 µg/L for Cu(II), Hg(II), and Pb(II), respectively110. CNTs, acting as conductive conduits within a chelating polymer matrix, enable very low LODs by markedly amplifying the faradaic response; however, such ultra-trace detection is often coupled to relatively narrow linear ranges, a trade-off that must be weighed against the concentration window of the intended application. ZnO NPs decorated with MWCNT electrodes enabled simultaneous and sensitive electrochemical quantification of Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) in water samples by SWV using the spike recovery method. Linear ranges of 1–800 µg/L, 5–1000 µg/L, and 1–900 µg/L were achieved, with LOD (3σ method) of 0.8, 1.2, and 0.7 ppb for Cu(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), respectively, determined in simulated deionized water. The electrode demonstrated approximately three to five times the sensitivity compared with the unmodified counterpart, accompanied by sharp, well-resolved peaks and negligible overlap from Co(II), Mn(II), and Fe(III), yielding recoveries ranging from 95% to 102%111. However, the lack of data on signal decay under repeated use or in the presence of organic foulants means that their field-readiness remains only partially established. Furthermore, Zn(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Cu(II), and Hg(II) were simultaneously detected by SWV using a GCE functionalized with a Fe3O4/fluorinated MWCNT nanocomposite, delivering clear peak profiles with enhanced sensitivity. Optimized electrochemical parameters yielded LOD (3σ method) of 1.57 µg/L for Cd(II), 1.74 µg/L for Pb(II), 0.78 µg/L for Hg(II), 0.78 µg/L for Zn(II), and 0.34 µg/L for Cu(II) in spiked purified water. The sensor remained stable for at least 30 days, a performance attributed to the negatively polarized C–F groups, the conductive architecture, and the synergistic interaction between Fe3O4 and CNTs112. The 30-day stability data represent a valuable reference point for long-term performance reporting and provide a useful template that future studies across different nanomaterial platforms should aim to match or exceed. Compared with graphene, CNT-based sensors can deliver similar levels of signal amplification, yet uniform dispersion and reproducible film morphology are typically more difficult to achieve.

As zero-dimensional nanomaterials typically below 10 nm, carbon and graphene QDs are increasingly employed as functional modifiers, owing to their high surface functionality and compatibility with biological environments. QDs have been effectively applied in environmental fields, drug delivery, light-emitting technologies, biosensing, anti-counterfeiting, water monitoring, and food analysis113. Compared with their bulk equivalents, QDs exhibit tunable electronic and optical characteristics controlled through heteroatom doping (N, S, B, P, or Se)114. For example, Heteroatoms N, O, and S-doped carbon QDs were used to identify and quantify copper in water samples113. Furthermore, N-doped graphene QDs showed superior selectivity toward Hg(II) over a broad range of competing metal ions, including Cd(II), Zn(II), Cu(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Ba(II), Mn(II), Pb(II), Cr(III), Al(III), and Fe(III). Under these conditions, a LOD of 10 µg/L for Hg(II) was achieved in synthetic ultrapure water using the S/N ratio of 3 method115. The strong Hg(II) selectivity of the sensor across such a broad ion panel illustrates how heteroatom doping can create favorable coordination sites; however, the LOD of 10 µg/L remains modest compared with gold- or bismuth-based sensors, underscoring that selectivity and sensitivity do not necessarily track and must be balanced according to the intended application. Notably, the literature documents that Graphene QDs doped Au NPs were successfully detected Cu and Hg by ASV, with exceptionally low LODs (S/N ratio method) of 3.2 × 10−3, 4.0 × 10−3 µg/L, respectively116. The synergy between QDs and Au NPs, where QDs increase the density of binding sites and Au NPs promote amalgam formation and facilitate electron transfer, illustrates how hybridization can drive LODs into the sub-ppb regime, although the cost and fouling susceptibility of the gold component must be carefully weighed in any field-deployment scenario.

Furthermore, a recent study reported that a graphene QDs /porphyrin composite can act as a sensor for monitoring Cd(II) and Cu(II) ions in seawater by SWV without any pre-treatment, achieving LOD of 49.0 and 10.9 µg/L, respectively, in simulated purified water using the 3σ method117. The ability to detect seawater without pre-treatment is a significant step toward field deployability, but the LODs are above many regulatory guideline values, revealing a remaining gap between operational simplicity and analytical sensitivity in complex saline matrices. Zn(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) were detected by a nanocomposite of graphene QDs and chitosan via SWV without interfering with K, Na, and Al(III) ions, with LODs 8.83, 3.11, and 1.99 µg/L118. Graphene QDs decorated graphene functioned as a simple yet effective ASV platform for Cu(II) determination in real water samples, providing results comparable to those obtained by AAS. It afforded a linear response from 0.95 to 557 µg/L and a detection limit (S/N = 3) of 0.083 µg/L in simulated deionized water, with negligible interference from a wide range of organic molecules CHCl3, CHBr3, C6H5Cl, C6Cl5OH, CH2Cl2 and metal ions including Zn(II), Cd(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Hg(II), Mn(II), Pb(II), Fe(III), Al(III), Na, K, and Sn(II)119. The interference study testing metal ions at 15-fold and organic substances at 100-fold excess relative to Cu(II) is commendably comprehensive and sets a useful standard for selectivity evaluation; the high tolerance under such conditions supports strong anti-fouling performance against these species, although direct comparison with their typical environmental concentrations would further strengthen the field-relevance assessment.

CNFs constitute a class of one-dimensional nanomaterials characterized by a stacked-cup or platelet arrangement, facilitating the exposure of abundant accessible edge-plane sites120. These sites are electrochemically more active than the basal planes found in CNTs or graphene. The electrochemical sensing capabilities of CNFs are attributable to their large surface area, chemically tunable surface functionality, high electrical conductivity, structural stability, and compatibility with multi-material integration120,121,122. Compared with CNTs, CNFs often present a higher proportion of edge-plane-like sites per unit mass, which can confer favorable heterogeneous electron-transfer kinetics even in the absence of extensive post-synthesis doping or functionalization.

For instance, CNFs decorated by CuO were utilized to detect three different classes of contaminants in real tap water samples, including heavy metals, antibiotics, and pesticides (tylosin tartrate, chloramphenicol, imidacloprid, quinalphos, lead, and cadmium) via DPV123. The combination of bis(indolyl)methane and mesoporous CNFs on a GCE has been utilized as an ASV sensor for Hg(II) quantification in river water, providing results comparable to those obtained by AAS. It delivers excellent selectivity and sensitivity in multi-metal matrices containing Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cu(II), achieving a detection limit of 0.06 µg/L in simulated purified water using the 3σ method124. The sub-ppb LOD positions CNF-based sensors competitively against Au-electrode systems, with the added advantages of lower material cost and excellent storage stability (92% signal retention after four weeks); however, the long-term operational stability under repeated oxidative stripping cycles, a prerequisite for reusable field sensors, is not reported, leaving a gap in the assessment of practical usability. Another reported sensor design combines bimetallic PtAu with CNFs for the simultaneous SWASV detection of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cu(II), achieving a lowest detected concentration (corresponding to 11.2 µg/L, 20.7 µg/L, and 6.4 µg/L) with relative standard deviations of 4-8%125. The use of PtAu NPs introduces a clear cost burden; while the sensor offers good repeatability and simultaneous multi-element detection, the absence of cost-normalized benchmarking against monometallic or metal-free CNF systems leaves the economic case for such noble-metal hybrids in routine monitoring unresolved.

Vertically aligned CNFs have also been applied for Pb(II) determination by ASV, achieving a LOD of 0.35 µg/L based on the 3σ method, highlighting their strong capability for trace lead analysis in aqueous samples126. The application of a carbon paste electrode incorporating a polypyrrole/CNF nanocomposite enabled trace detection of Pb(II) in real water samples, with a detection limit of 0.05 µg/L (3σ method) determined in spiked purified water and excellent linearity across the tested range127. A recent publication presented a screen-printed sensor architecture featuring CNFs functionalized with Fe3O4 NPs alongside MWCNT bearing Fe3O4 NPs to simultaneously detect Cd(II), Pb(II), Hg(II), and Cu(II), where the Fe3O4/CNF modified sensor outperformed the Fe3O4/ MWCNT variant in terms of detection limit (for example, 3.2 and 66 µg/L for Pb(II)), respectively128. This direct head-to-head comparison provides evidence that CNFs can outperform MWCNTs in electrochemical sensing, which the authors link to more uniform Fe3O4 dispersion on the CNF scaffold, improved structural stability, and a more favorable synergy between components.

Integrating metal and metal oxide nanostructures into electrochemical platforms has advanced the sensitivity and selectivity of voltammetric heavy metal measurements, addressing their relevance in environmental monitoring and exposure control. These nanostructured materials exhibit markedly enhanced catalytic activity together with a higher electroactive surface area (EASA) than bulk counterparts, thereby accelerating electron-transfer reactions and facilitating the detection of heavy metal ions at ultra-trace levels, spanning from parts per billion to parts per trillion129.

Noble and transition metal NPs serve widely as electrode modifiers, attributable to their superior conductive properties and their ability to form alloys or amalgams with heavy metal ions, which is critically important in stripping voltammetric measurements130. As an illustration, recent work has shown that bismuth nanoplates and bismuth/Nafion composites can detect Pb(II) and Cd(II) via ASV with LOD (3.3σ method) of approximately 0.037 and 0.042 µg/L, respectively, obtained under optimized conditions in an acetate buffer that simulates ultrapure water131. These sensors also enabled low-nanomolar Pb(II) and Cd(II) determination in spiked tap water (LODs ≈0.043 and 0.46 µg/L) and in industrial effluent (LODs ≈ 1.19 and 3.8 µg/L), confirming their practical relevance for real-sample analysis. Additionally, the sensor showed good selectivity toward Pb(II) and Cd(II) in the presence of multiple inorganics and organic interferents, 14-day stability with <10% signal variation, and batch-to-batch reproducibility of about 6–8% for n = 6. It's simple drop-casting/dipping fabrication, compatibility with portable potentiostats, and Nafion-based antifouling and cation-exchange properties support low-cost field deployability. Bismuth modified screen printed electrodes, and GCE enabled cathodic stripping voltammetric analysis of Rh, Pt, and Pd, reaching LOD of 0.005, 0.006, and 0.008 μg/L, respectively, in standard solutions and applied in water and sediment samples without interfering with Fe(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Na, SO42−, and phosphate PO43− 132. Selectivity is good for common ions, whereas Co(II) and Ni(II) significantly affect the signal when present at equal or higher concentrations, a limitation not observed for the Bi/Nafion Cd/Pb system. Stability was verified over 28 h with minimal loss of peak current, but longer-term data is missing. Reproducibility (RSD 5–9%) can be regarded as moderate.

Additionally, a porous Au electrode prepared on GCE using an Au-based Prussian blue analog sacrificial template has been employed for the detection of trace As(III) in water, with ASV and the standard addition method affording a LOD of 0.49 µg/L (S/N = 3) for the As(0)→As(III) peak in acidified solutions133. This electrode provides good real-sample performance in tap, spring, and river water, with recoveries of approximately 99–103%, 98.6–104%, and 98.2–101%, respectively, and associated RSDs of about 2.6–2.9%, 3.2–4.1%, and 3.4–3.7%. Long-term stability over 30 days (RSD < 4%) and inter-electrode reproducibility around 3% are excellent. However, selectivity can be compromised by Cu(II), which overlaps the As(0) → As(III) signal in 0.5 M H₂SO4; this interference can be mitigated by exploiting the second oxidation peak (As(III) → As(V)) or by operating in phosphate buffer, a clever workaround but still a practical constraint. Moreover, the fabrication requires co-electrodeposition of Au and the Prussian blue analog, followed by alkali/acid template removal, making it more complex and potentially more costly than simple drop-casting routes typically used for Bi- or carbon-based sensors, and thus less attractive for large-scale, low-cost production. Another example of Au NPs/GCE for quantifying As(III) with LOD (S/N ratio of 3 method) of 1.8 μg/L in a laboratory electrolyte that effectively simulates ultrapure water conditions134. This sensor is less sensitive than the porous Au electrode described above and, in the available report, lacks validation in real water matrices, long-term stability testing, and batch-to-batch reproducibility data. Fabrication by electrodeposition of Au nanoparticles in the presence of iodide is relatively straightforward, but the absence of real-sample evaluation means that fouling resistance and field deployability remain uncertain, so overall this system is less comprehensively characterized than other As(III) sensors in this section. Furthermore, Se(IV) and As(III) were simultaneously determined in water using an AuNP-modified GCE by ASV, achieving LODs of 0.22 and 0.15 µg/L, respectively (3σ method), in deionized water and validated against ICP-OES measurements using a t-test at the 95% confidence level135. This sensor offers excellent sub µg/L sensitivity for dual-analyte detection and shows good repeatability, with RSDs of 3.2% and 2.8% for Se(IV) and As(III), respectively. Selectivity was evaluated against common cations, with negligible effects from Mg2⁺, Ca2⁺, Na⁺, and K⁺ and only minimal interference from Cu(II) and Cd(II), causing about 6.25% peak enhancement and 2.3% peak suppression, respectively. Fabrication by simple electrodeposition of AuNPs onto GCE is straightforward and low-cost, and the successful agreement with ICP-OES for industrial effluent samples (5.23 vs 5.60 µg/L for As(III) and 5.16 vs 3.7 µg/L for Se(IV)) suggests good potential for on-site monitoring, although long-term stability and fouling resistance in more complex matrices were not explicitly investigated.

The Pt NPs-modified electrode served as a simple yet effective ASV sensor for As(III), affording a linear response from 40 to 7950 nM and a sensitivity of 0.356 mA ppb−1, with negligible interference from a comprehensive set of metal ions, particularly Cu(II), which showed minimal stripping signal at the As(III) oxidation potential136. Another representative noble metal system involves Pd NPs impregnated CPE for Hg(II) sensing, which achieves a LOD of 1.00 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water, a value compatible with US EPA drinking water standards. This sensor has been successfully applied to tap water and municipal wastewater samples using the standard addition method137. In another design, a PANT/green AgNP modified CPE enabled simultaneous Cd(II) and Pb(II) determination by SWV, with LODs of 0.09 and 0.05 µg/L (S/N = 3) in acetate buffer.
