The sensor combines sub-ppb sensitivity, acceptable seawater recoveries (92–114%), good stability (>92% signal retention over 14 days), and reproducible fabrication (RSD < 4.2%), while remaining low-cost and scalable, though long-term field robustness and fouling behavior were not fully assessed138. A GCE modified by casting Au/Pd NPs onto its surface has been employed for the ASV detection of As(III), achieving an LOD of 0.25 µg/L (S/N‑based) in 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.5), with negligible interference from Cd(II), Pb(II), Zn(II), and Mn(II)139. The Au–Pd/GCE sensor, therefore, offers good sensitivity and selectivity toward several co‑metal ions, but no real‑sample analysis is reported, which is a major gap for assessing performance in complex matrices. Cu(II) and Hg(II) cause significant interference, which can be alleviated by adding sulfate (0.06–0.08 M), and stability was only evaluated over 20 consecutive cycles without signal loss, with no information on long‑term storage stability or batch‑to‑batch reproducibility. Fabrication by casting pre‑synthesized Au–Pd core shell NPs is of moderate complexity and higher material cost (use of both Au and Pd), and in the absence of real‑sample validation, field deployability remains uncertain.

In a separate configuration, bimetallic Au/Pt were employed for the quantification of As(III) by ASV, in 0.5 M H₂SO4, giving LODs of 0.28 and 0.45 µg/L (S/N = 3) for the As(0) → As(III) and As(III) → As(V) peaks over linear ranges of 0.37–225 and 0.75–225 µg/L, respectively140. The design exploits mild hydrogen evolution at Pt sites to chemically reduce As(III) to As(0), enhancing cathodic preconcentration and yielding sharp dual stripping signals at neighboring Au sites. Real water analysis is reported, but the excerpt does not detail recoveries, long-term stability, or electrode-to-electrode reproducibility, so durability and batch scalability remain unclear. Fabrication by short-time co-electrodeposition of Au–Pt NPs is straightforward, though noble-metal cost (Au + Pt) is higher than for single-metal or non-noble systems. Au/Bi bimetallic nanoparticle-functionalized GO nanocomposites were fabricated and applied for the selective quantification of Fe(III) in seawater and lake samples using the standard addition method. The sensor exhibited a linear response over 11.2–2,790 µg/L with a LOD of 3.9 µg/L (S/N = 3) determined in in 0.1 M HCl, without significant interference from K, Na, Pb(II), Zn(II), Mn(II), Ca, Cu(II), Al(III), or Mg ions, and was successfully applied to lake and seawater, with recoveries of ~90–103% and RSD < 2.7% when validated against ICP-MS141.

The family of metal oxides, including TiO2, Fe3O4, Fe2O3, CuO, ZnO, SnO2, and CeO2, represents another core class of nanoparticle modifiers for heavy metal sensing platforms142,143. Nanoparticles exhibit several practical benefits, notably magnetic behavior that simplifies electrode modification with high chemical stability and plentiful surface hydroxyl functionalities for analyte binding144,145.

For instance, Fe3O4 NPs were used to detect Hg(II), Cu(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) via ASV in river water samples, with stability over 15 cycles, giving RSD 4.9%, and spiked river water recovery was 91.5%146. Fe₂O3 nanorods and hollow nanocubes (both derived from Prussian blue) were compared as GCE modifiers for ASV detection of Pb(II) in aqueous solution. Despite the hollow nanocubes having a larger BET surface area, Fe₂O3 nanorods exposing the (010) plane delivered much higher sensitivity (109.67 µA mM−1) and a lower LOD of 0.70 µg/L than the hollow nanocubes (17.68 µA mM−1, LOD 17.2 µg/L), underscoring that specific adsorption sites (Fe–O) dominate over total surface area in governing electroanalytical performance. The nanorod-modified GCE was successfully applied to spiked real water samples, giving Pb(II) recoveries of 92–108% with RSD ≤ 8.4%147. Fabrication (etching + calcination) is moderately complex but uses low-cost precursors. Scalability is reasonable, though the multi-step synthesis increases cost relative to drop-cast nanoparticles. In another recent study, TiO2 Nanorods were used for the simultaneous identification of Cr(III), Hg(II), and Cu(II) ions with notable high sensitivity of 15.6, 19.7and 92.2 µAmM−1 cm−2 and LODs of about 1.13 × 106, 5.72 × 106, and 2.35 × 106 µg/L, respectively143. The TiO₂ electrode shows good reliability (small current variation across five electrodes) and retains ~92% of its initial Cu2⁺ sensitivity over 30 days, but its relatively high LODs restrict use to relatively contaminated samples rather than ultra-trace monitoring.

ZnO nanofiber-coated GCE has been applied as a sensing platform for Cd(II) determination with SWV, with a linear response range between 0.54 and 146 µg/L and LOD (S/N method) of 0.20 µg/L that was determined in 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 4.6). The electrode shows markedly higher Cd(II) stripping currents than bare, Nafion-coated, or ZnO NP modified GCEs, consistent with enhanced adsorption via the high-area nanofiber network and dense surface hydroxyl groups, and was successfully applied to Cd determination in real water samples, with simultaneous multi-metal (Cd, Pb, Cu, Hg) analysis also demonstrated148. In separate configurations, a CeO2 NPs fabricated via a simple sucrose/nitrate combustion route was used for ASV quantification of Zn(II) in 0.1 M acetate buffer (pH 6), giving a linear range of 20–380 µg/L and an LOD of 0.36 µg/L. The CeO₂ film (10–12 nm particles, mesoporous, ~18 m2/g) greatly enhances Zn(II) oxidation currents relative to bare GCE through increased active area and adsorption, and the sensor shows good selectivity against common cations/anions, with real industrial effluents (battery and metal processing wastewaters) yielding recoveries near 99–101% and RSD < 4%149. Fabrication (combustion synthesis followed by drop-casting) is simple, low-cost, and in principle scalable. The straightforward modification step supports good potential for field deployment, although actual on-site trials are not reported. Fouling resistance was not explicitly investigated.

Nanocomposite of SnO2/rGO doped with Co/Fe was used for selective detection of Cr (VI) and Cd (II) ions via CV, giving linear responses from about 0.07 to 50 µg/L with LODs of 0.07 and 0.04 µg/L detected in simulated high-quality deionized water, respectively, well below WHO guideline values150. The doped SnO₂/rGO architectures significantly enhance sensitivity (up to ~3.2 µA/µg·L−1 for Cd and ~9.4 µA/µg·L−1 for Cr) relative to bare SnO₂ or rGO, while maintaining good selectivity against most common ions and retaining ~92% of the initial signal after 30 days of storage. Real-matrix testing is limited to prepared aqueous solutions, leaving a major gap in evaluating matrix effects. Interference studies show moderate signal perturbation from Hg(II) and As(III) in Cr(VI) sensing and from Pb(II) in Cd(II) sensing, whereas Na(I), K(I), Mn(II), Zn(II), Mg(II), Ca(II), and Al(III) have negligible impact. Fabrication via hydrothermal synthesis plus ultrasonication is multi-step, though it uses low-cost precursors, giving moderate scalability. Another study reported the detection of Cd(II), Hg(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II) ions using a 3D interlayer rGO/SnO₂/polypyrrole nanohybrid (rGO from expanded graphite, SnO₂ nanoparticles, polypyrrole overlayer) via ASV. The method exhibited excellent sensitivity and current density, with LODs (3σ method) of 8.4 × 10⁻⁵, 1.6 × 10⁻⁴, 5.3 × 10⁻⁵, 1.6 × 10⁻⁴, and 1.8 × 10⁻⁴ µg/L that were detected in simulated high-quality deionized water, respectively151. The study reports no real-sample tests, leaving matrix effects and practical performance unassessed. Interference data for this specific hybrid, as well as long-term stability and batch-to-batch reproducibility, are all absent. Fabrication by hydrothermal synthesis followed by in-situ polymerization is relatively complex and multi-step, which may hinder scale-up.

Simultaneous detection of trace Tl(I) and Hg(II) in water samples by SnO2/MWCNTs Modified GCE via ASV with a limit of quantification (3σ method, 3.0 × 10−3 and 4.0 × 10−3 µg/L) and detection ranged from 0.003 to 450 and 0.004 to 400 µg/L, respectively152. The sensor shows low RSDs (≈2–4%) for repeatability/reproducibility, retains its response over three weeks, and delivers 96.7–99.1% recoveries in spiked surface waters, with results consistent with ICP-MS. Fabrication (casting of nanocomposite) is straightforward and relatively low-cost, as it uses commercially available MWCNTs. The simple platform, ng/L level LODs, multiweek stability, and successful surface water measurements indicate good potential for field deployment, though dedicated on-site trials are not reported. Fouling resistance was not directly evaluated, but stable currents and accurate recoveries in surface waters imply acceptable matrix tolerance.

As a new generation of functional materials, MOFs and COFs have recently been recognized as advanced platforms for voltammetric heavy-metal ion detection, owing to their remarkable architectural diversity and finely adjustable functional sites. MOFs possess crystalline porous frameworks constructed from metal ions or clusters bridged by organic linkers, providing both abundant active sites and a significant internal surface area, facilitating the accumulation of analytes153,154. Likewise, COFs are crystalline porous frameworks formed from light elements (C, H, N, O, B) connected via strong covalent interactions, delivering exceptional chemical durability and well-defined pore size and structural geometry155. A broad range of COF materials, including triazine- and imine-linked architectures, feature an ordered porous network that facilitates selective diffusion of metal ions, while their inherently modular design permits the installation of functional groups (amino, thiol, carboxyl, etc.) to enhance coordination efficiency and selectivity156,157. Furthermore, MOF- and COF-derived composites such as carbonaceous materials and metal /metal oxide NPs hybrids harness beneficial structural features from the parent frameworks that translate into enhanced electrical conductivity and catalytic behavior, and their synergistic coupling with conductive nanomaterials or supports resolves the limitation of poor native conductivity of the pristine frameworks and improves rapid charge transfer at the electrode interface153,154,155,156,157. Consequently, these frameworks serve simultaneously as pre-concentrating adsorbents and as active sensing phases, thereby collectively enhancing voltammetric sensitivity and selectivity153,154.

Recent work demonstrated that the UiO-66-NH2(Zr)/GO nanocomposite drop-cast on GCE (Nafion binder) has been successfully demonstrated as a selective voltammetric probe for Cu(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II) via ASV, affording LODs (3σ method) of 0.59, 2.9, and 0.84 µg/L in an acetate buffer (pH 4.5)158. The MOF/GO hybrid (one-pot hydrothermal growth of UiO-66-NH₂ on GO) provides high sensitivity of 1.30 µA µM−1 for Cu(II), 0.50 µA µM−1 for Cd(II), and 12.38 µA µM−1 for Pb(II), and the sensor was successfully applied to lake, river, tap water, sediment extracts, and wastewater at µg/L levels with recoveries ≥85% and ≥85% signal reproducibility, indicating good stability and practical applicability in diverse environmental matrices. While the sensor is, in principle, compatible with portable potentiostats for field use, on-site fouling behavior and true field-ready performance remain untested in this study.

A novel electrochemical sensor based on NH2-MIL-88(Fe)/g-C3N4/CNT was drop-cast on GCE (no Nafion binder) and designed for the trace determination of Cd(II), Pb(II), Hg(II) and Cu(II) in drinking-water via SWV with high sensitivity of 0.0789, 0.4122, 0.3251, and 0.2616 mA μM−1 cm−2 and LODs of 4.45, 1.58, 1.93, and 0.76 µg/L in acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 5.4), respectively159. The electrode shows good selectivity against large excesses of common ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Mg2⁺, Ca2⁺, Zn2⁺, Co2⁺, Ni2⁺, Fe3⁺, Ag⁺), short-term stability with RSDs of 1.6–7.0% over 10 consecutive runs, and inter-electrode reproducibility with RSDs of 4.4–11.4%, and it achieves 80–108% recoveries for all four metals in real sewage samples from a municipal wastewater treatment plant but lack fouling resistance data. Compared with the UiO-66-NH₂(Zr)/GO ASV sensor (sub-µg/L LODs in varied environmental waters), the NH₂-MIL-88(Fe) composite SWV platform adds Hg(II), has somewhat higher LODs but higher areal sensitivities for Pb2⁺/Cu2⁺/Hg2⁺, and employs a binder-free, one-pot Fe-MOF/carbon composite that simplifies fabrication at the expense of slightly higher RSDs.

The integration of Au NPs within CuBTC MOFs has been reported as an excellent sensing platform, showing great affinity and selectivity toward Pb(II) ions and achieved a LOD of 0.207 µg/L by DPV technique in simulated pure water, which is far below EPA MCL in drinking water regulations160. Through the synergistic combination of porous CuBTC adsorption and Au-catalyzed redox, with linear response from 0.207 to 2.07 × 10⁶ µg/L and selectivity over Cr(III) with a critical gap of no real-sample testing. The integration of a flower-like NiSe/ZIF-67 nanozyme has been highlighted as an efficient dual mode sensing platform, exhibiting strong affinity and selectivity toward Hg(II), Pb(II), and Cd(II), providing broad linear ranges in both colorimetric and electrochemical modes and achieving LODs (3σ method) down to 1.24, 0.97, and 0.58 µg/L in simulated pure water, with wide linear ranges spanning roughly 58–30,800 µg/L, 62–73,700 µg/L, 3.3–86,000 µg/L respectively, thereby demonstrating its suitability for real environmental samples following the regular addition method161. The NiSe/ZIF/GCE exhibits good precision, with intra- and inter-electrode RSDs of a few percent and yields 95–105% recoveries for all three ions in spiked natural waters, in close agreement with ICP-OES results (RSD ≲ 6%), demonstrating practical accuracy in real matrices. Fabrication by hydrothermal synthesis of NiSe, solvothermal ZIF-67 growth, and simple dispersion/drop-casting onto GCE is relatively straightforward and scalable, making the electrochemical mode of this sensor practically attractive even without using its auxiliary colorimetric readout. The synthesis and application of a UiO-66(Zr)-based MOF electrode decorated with Ag2O NPs have been explored for the determination of Cd(II) and Hg(II), achieving LODs (3σ method) of 0.90 and 0.60 µg/L with high sensitivities of 551 and 341.2 μA μM−1 cm−2, respectively, and the sensor has been successfully applied to complex environmental matrices, including soil, wasteland runoff, river basin water, and groundwater samples spiked with Cd(II) and Hg(II)162. The fabrication protocol is practical for routine use, relying on conventional UiO-66 synthesis, in situ generation of Ag₂O within the MOF pores via post-synthetic modification, and simple casting of a Nafion/carbon black–based ink onto commercial ITO slides, which supports low-cost and scalable electrode preparation for heavy-metal monitoring. A recent study has focused on the integration of methylene blue functionalized UiO-66-NH2 with MWCNTs for sensitive and selective determination of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II), demonstrating LODs (S/N = 3) of 1.25, 1.13, and 1.29 µg/L, under simultaneous detection mode, while individual determinations reached down to roughly 0.44, 0.14, and 0.064 µg/L, respectively. The sensor has been effectively applied to the simultaneous detection of these ions in real water and herbal medicine samples, with results in good agreement with those obtained by ICP-MS163. The incorporation of UiO-66-NH₂ provides a high surface area and abundant adsorption sites, whereas MWCNTs and the ratiometric MB signal enhance conductivity, robustness, and signal normalization, enabling practical application to lake water and concentrated herbal extracts with good analytical performance.

Additionally, Ta-BTDC-COF/SPE was used with DPV for Pb(II) and As(V) in water, giving linear ranges of 0.5–1000 µg/L for Pb(II) and 25–10,000 µg/L for As(V), with LODs of 0.035 and 0.42 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water, respectively, highlighting the advantages of unique COF morphologies in mass transport. The COF, rich in N and S sites, provides strong coordination and electrostatic binding of the target ions, enabling high sensitivity alongside good selectivity: most metal ions and anions at 100-fold excess cause <10% signal change, with only CO32− noticeably affecting As(V). The sensor shows excellent practicality, retaining stable responses over 6 weeks (RSD ≈ 6%), delivering inter-electrode RSDs of ~4–10%, and achieving 96–106% recoveries for Pb(II) and As(V) in deep well, tap, and industrial wastewater samples, with good agreement to ICP-OES and even detecting Pb at levels below the ICP-OES detection limit164. Recent research has reported the synthesis of a triazine-based COF (solvothermal for 3 days, 120 °C), drop-cast) used as an electrochemical modifier for the sensitive and selective simultaneous detection of Cd(II) and Pb(II) at nanomolar levels via ASV, providing linear ranges of 0.21–22.8 µg/L for Pb(II) and 0.56–33.7 µg/L for Cd(II), with limits of detection (3σ method) of 0.23 and 0.20 µg/L in spiked doubly distilled water, respectively. The sensor was applied effectively to determine Pb(II) and Cd(II) ions in water and food samples, including white rice, drinking water, green tea, marine salt, sugar, dried powdered spices, and black tea, providing results comparable to those obtained by ICP/MS165.

The evolution of advanced electrochemical sensors for heavy metal monitoring has been substantially driven by the incorporation of polymeric systems, notably CPs and MIPs. Such materials possess high electrical conductivity alongside flexible structural adaptability and compatibility with selective recognition functionalization. CPs promote fast electron transfer across the electrode interface as effective transducers, whereas MIPs ensure selective binding through engineered recognition sites that mirror the size, morphology, and functional chemistry of the target ions166,167,168. In addition, the combination of facile synthesis and precise modulation of polymer properties via electrochemical or chemical routes positions these materials as ideal candidates for portable and affordable sensor development.

polypyrrole (PPy), PANI, and polythiophene (PTh), representative CPs, feature delocalized \({\boldsymbol{\pi }}\)-electron frameworks that facilitate efficient charge transport and yield numerous active sites suitable for interaction with analytes169. Their suitability for voltammetric detection is primarily associated with their reversible redox behavior and the strong chelating interactions that imine, amine, and pyrrolic nitrogen groups exhibit toward heavy metal ions170,171. A novel PANI/yellow 42 dye composite was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) and Pb(II) via DPV, providing a linear response over (Hg(II): 200.6–4212.4 μg/L, Pb(II): 207.2–4351.2 μg/L) with low LODs (S/N = 3, Hg(II): 0.40 µg/L, Pb(II): 1.28 µg/L) in spiked double distilled water172. Clear peaks remained well resolved in the presence of common cations, evidencing high selectivity and strong anti-interference behavior. Real-matrix robustness was demonstrated in lake and groundwater samples using standard addition, giving recoveries of roughly 96.6–103.0% for Pb(II) and 92.3–105.0% for Hg(II) with low RSDs, while repeatability and batch reproducibility were confirmed by RSDs of about 1.2% (same electrode, ten consecutive runs) and 1.8% (five independently prepared electrodes), respectively. The PANI/dye chemistry and simple chemical oxidative polymerization followed by drop-casting onto commercial GCEs make fabrication scalable and low-cost, and the DPV readout on a conventional three-electrode cell is inherently compatible with portable potentiostats for field deployment, although fouling resistance, very long-term stability in complex waters, and any systematic cost analysis of per-sensor materials and processing have not been explicitly addressed in this work.

A recent paper utilized the integration of PANI into a sodium alginate matrix then drop-cast on SPCE as a DPV sensor, showing high sensitivity and selectivity toward Pb(II) and Cd(II) with sensitivity of 3.183 μA μM−1 cm−2 for Cd(II) at 10 µL loading roughly an eightfold improvement over 0.394 µA µM⁻1 cm⁻2 at 5 µL, thus highlighting its promise for low-level heavy-metal monitoring in environmental waters173.

The biopolymer conducting polymer matrix affords high selectivity (clear separation of Pb(II) and Cd(II) peaks even at 100 µM Cd(II) background), good storage stability (≈97% of the initial current retained after two weeks at ambient conditions), and reproducible fabrication, as evidenced by consistent responses obtained with multiple independently prepared electrodes. The in-situ oxidative polymerization of PANI in an abundant, low cost alginate matrix followed by simple drop-casting onto commercially available SPEs is inherently scalable and inexpensive and is readily compatible with portable potentiostats for field deployment, although the study does not examine fouling resistance in real waters, long-term operation beyond two weeks, real-matrix recoveries, or any systematic cost analysis per sensor, which would be needed to fully substantiate field-ready performance.

The synthesis and application of decorated PPy with polymerized phytic acid disposable sensor have been explored for the trace detection of Pb(II) via ASV in real tap water samples in a linear range of 2.07–124 µg/L, achieving LOD of 0.09 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water with good repeatability, stability, and anti-interference behavior174. The sensor film improves hydrophilicity (contact angle ~32° vs 133.5° for bare SPCE), chelates Pb ions through phosphate groups, and yields enhanced stripping peaks compared to PPy and bare SPCE, while selectivity tests show that most common ions at 10-fold excess cause <±10% signal change; significant interference from Hg(II) and Cu(II) can be mitigated using masking agents. The sensor demonstrates good operational robustness, with repeatability over 20 successive ASV runs (RSD < 2%), storage stability over several days (overall stability RSD ≈ 3.1%), and inter-electrode reproducibility assessed on five independently prepared SPCEs, plus real-sample applicability confirmed by tap-water spike tests giving 93.2–98.9% recoveries and close agreement with ICP-MS. However, PPy decorated with NiFe₂O4 shows a comparatively higher LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.81 µg/L for Pb(II) by ASV in tap, lake, and river water using the standard addition method, offering a linear range of 20.7–435 µg/L with a sensitivity of 15.870 µA µM−1 cm−2, which is more than twice as sensitive as bare GCE (4.426 µA µM−1 cm−2) and clearly outperforms NiFe₂O4 alone. Real-matrix robustness is demonstrated by satisfactory recoveries of 98–107% in various aqueous media, and interference tests show that 10–20-fold excess of Cd(II), Fe(III), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), and Na produces only minor current changes, while simultaneous ASV reveals distinct peaks for Pb(II) vs Cd(II)/Cu(II), confirming high selectivity175. The sensing mechanism combines increased surface area and redox-active Fe/Ni (II/III) cycling in NiFe₂O4 with PPy's N-donor sites to enhance adsorption and charge transfer, but explicit long-term stability, fouling resistance, batch-to-batch reproducibility, fabrication cost, and systematic field deployability analyses are not reported for this system. Between these two ASV platforms, the phytic-acid-functionalized phytic-PPy/SPCE achieves a lower LOD and more rigorously quantified operational robustness, whereas the NiFe₂O4/PPy/GCE affords broader real-matrix coverage, a wider linear range, and mechanistically supported, highly selective Pb(II) recognition, but lacks equally detailed stability and deployability assessment.

The integration of PTh with Pt/g-C3N4 nanocomposite drop-cast on GCE has been highlighted as a sensing platform, exhibiting strong affinity and selectivity toward Hg(II) providing a broad linear range of 0.201–100.3 µg/L and achieving LOD (S/N = 3) down to 1.81 × 10⁻3 µg/L and sensitivity of 1.0787 μAnM−1 cm−2, thereby demonstrating its suitability for real wastewater samples. The sensor that decorated with Pt nanoparticles furnishes abundant active sites and high conductivity, exhibited negligible cross-response for Hg(II) in the presence of interfering species, remaining unchanged in the presence of K(II) and Mn(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Pd(II), Sn(II), Zn(II), Ag(I), Cu(II), and Fe(III) at concentrations 100-fold higher than Hg176. Real-matrix robustness is evidenced by recovery tests at 0.20, 40.12 and 80.24 µg/L Hg(II) in mixed electrolyte and by wastewater analysis, where results agree well with AAS and repeated measurements (n = 6) at each level display acceptable RSD values, indicating good short-term stability and reusability without pronounced fouling. The synthesis route thermal preparation of low-cost g-C3N4 followed by solution-phase reduction of Pt(II) with L-cysteine and in situ PTh formation relies on standard wet-chemistry and drop-casting steps that are inherently scalable, but the use of Pt inevitably raises per-sensor material cost, and the study does not provide detailed batch-to-batch statistics, long-term stability in real effluents, or explicit field-deployment trials, which remain to be addressed for practical large-scale monitoring.

MIPs, widely termed “synthetic antibodies,” have contributed a biomimetic paradigm for selective recognition that has influenced voltammetric heavy-metal detection. In preparing MIPs, functional monomers are polymerized around a template species, followed by template leaching to create defined cavities whose shape, size, and chemical functionality. In the context of detecting heavy metals, these materials are frequently termed ion-imprinted polymers (IIPs), with selectivity governed by the ordered distribution of functional groups within the polymer matrix, promoting high-affinity complexation of target ions and limiting nonspecific binding. Figure 6 schematically outlines the IIP synthesis process, covering template–monomer association and polymerization, subsequent template removal, and the selective rebinding of target metal ions to the imprinted cavities. Figure 7 illustrates an alternative preconcentration strategy based on a Nafion-protected metal (Bi, Sb, or Sn) film electrode, where Pb(II) and Cd(II) diffuse through the cation-exchange membrane and are reduced into alloy films, while organic foulants are blocked at the Nafion interface, enabling stripping voltammetric detection without the need for molecular imprinting. Additionally, IIP/nanomaterial hybrids integrated with metal NPs, graphene, and CNTs provide a practical route to mitigate the intrinsically low electronic conductivity of conventional polymer matrices. A representative approach involves a Pb(II) selective IIP integrated with Fe3O4 and SiO2 for DPV-based trace detection in fruit juice and natural water matrices, where 4-vinylpyridine, 2-(2-aminophenyl)-1H-benzimidazole, and Pb(NO3)2 served as monomer, ligand, and template, respectively, and Pb particles were subsequently leached using HCl. Fe3O4@SiO2@IIP delivered high selectivity in determination of Pb(II) even in the presence diverse competing species up to a 500-fold excess, notably Ca(II), Mg(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cr(III), Ag(I), Hg(II), Na(I), K(I), Cd(II), and Cu(II), reaching LOD of 0.050 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water and linear range of 0.1-80 µg/L with two well-defined calibration segments that maintain high correlation (R2 ≈ 0.99), thereby demonstrating its suitability for real food and water samples following the regular addition method177. Real-matrix robustness is demonstrated by successful application to tap, rain, and river waters and fruit juice, with recoveries between 95.0 and 105.0% and RSD values typically below 5.5%, confirming accurate Pb(II) quantification in chemically diverse samples under optimized pH, deposition potential, and time conditions obtained via Box–Behnken design. A further study demonstrated selective Hg(II) determination via ASV in aqueous samples using IIP derived from pyrrole as the functional monomer, with ethylene glycol dimethacrylate and sodium persulfate serving as the cross-linker and initiator, respectively. The pristine pyrrole IIP exhibited a detection limit of 20.0 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water and demonstrated acceptable intra-day and inter-day precision at two concentration levels (%RSD < 10%) in spiked real water samples processed without acid digestion178. A recent paper utilized an IIP targeting As(III) synthesized from 2-methylthiophene and methacrylic acid as functional monomers, with ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linking agent, delivering an LOD of 7.00 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water and recovery close to 99.7% in spiked commercial, dam, and tap water samples regardless of acid digestion179. In a separate configuration, ion-imprinted chitosan-graphene electrode was utilized to electrochemically detect Cr(VI) in tap water and river water following the regular addition method with a low LOD of 0.03 µg/L using the S/N ratio of 3 method in spiked pure water and a linear range of 0.052 – 520 µg/L in the presence of interfering ions such as Co(II), Zn(II), Cu(II), Mn(II), Ni(II), C2O42−, MnO4−, MoO42− and S2O62− ions. Fabrication of the IIP by a single electrodeposition step resulted in strong repeatability and stability, maintaining 85% of its sensitivity after nine rebinding removal cycles and 87% signal retention after 17 days180. A Co(II)-selective electrochemical sensor was fabricated for aqueous samples through electrochemical polymerization of o-aminophenol, yielding a Co(II)-IIP film via DPV. This sensor revealed LOD of 0.094 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water and linearity range of 0.11–0.88 µg/L in the presence of competing ions including Cr(III), Cd(II), Hg(II), Ni(II), Zn(II), Mn(II), and ions181.

Schematic illustration of IIP fabrication and selective recognition, where the template metal ion (e.g., Pb(II)), functional monomers, and crosslinker/initiator form coordination complexes prior to polymerization. A crosslinked network around the template ions is formed after polymerization, followed by leaching with an acid or chelating agent to remove the template and generate complementary binding cavities. The target metal ions selectively re-enter the imprinted cavities, whereas competing ions (e.g., Ca(II), Mg(II)) are largely excluded. IIPs achieve 3–30× higher selectivity than non-imprinted polymers. This Figure was created using BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://BioRender.com/kqlsteo).

During the preconcentration step, Pb(II) and Cd(II) diffuse through the Nafion cation-exchange layer, are reduced at the electrode surface, and form Bi–M (or Sb–M, Sn–M) alloys within the metal film. The Nafion coating effectively blocks organic foulants and surfactants present in the bulk solution, preventing electrode fouling. The subsequent stripping step oxidizes the metals back into solution, producing characteristic voltammetric peaks proportional to the initial analyte concentration. This Figure was created using BioRender. Publication rights were acquired under a paid subscription in 2026. (https://BioRender.com/faknmzg).

The trend towards “green” analytical chemistry has stimulated the search for eco-friendly materials in electrochemical sensing, focusing on reducing the ecological footprint of traditional electrode manufacturing. The driving force behind this transition is the demand for affordable, renewable, and non-toxic substitutes for noble metals and synthetic carbon nanomaterials. Sustainable electrode materials, particularly those sourced from biomass and agricultural waste, feature a unique combination of structural diversity, natural heteroatom doping, and large surface area, which are essential for selectively and accurately monitoring heavy metal ions.

The process of converting lignocellulosic waste into functional carbon frameworks via hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis, or chemical activation techniques preserves the intrinsic porous structures and the inclusion of naturally occurring heteroatoms (such as O, N, S, and P). These attributes facilitate the pre-concentration of heavy metal ions via a combination of physical adsorption, surface complexation, and ion exchange processes. In addition, the graphitization, along with oxygen-containing functional groups present in biomass-derived carbon materials, further enhances electron transfer rates and provides a reliable interface for stripping analysis182. The conceptualization of biomass-derived electrodes has transitioned from simple waste valorization to the strategic design of hierarchical pore structures and surface chemistries targeting specific analytes. By leveraging the synergistic advantages of biomass-derived carbons alongside other green modifiers, including non-toxic metal films and natural polymers, researchers are innovating high-performance sensors that compete with traditional platforms in terms of sensitivity and selectivity, supporting both environmental sustainability and circular economy practices183,184.

A novel technique has been described for the simultaneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped biochar loaded with RuO2 sourced from cotton husks that is drop-cast on GCE. The resulting nanocomposite was used to achieve Pb(II) and Cu(II) voltammetric detection, with a linear dynamic range established as (Pb(II): 1.30 × 103–4.14 × 10⁴ µg/L, Cu(II): 3.97 × 102–1.27 × 10⁴ µg/L), with an LOD of 10.6 and 2.48 µg/L in spiked pure water, respectively185. High surface area biochar matrix combined with uniformly distributed RuO₂ nanoclusters, which enhances adsorption and charge transfer and yields well-defined DPV peaks for Pb(II) and Cu(II) that remain stable in the presence of common cations, evidencing good selectivity and anti-interference behavior. Real-matrix robustness and short-term stability are confirmed in Violatianshanica Maxim extracts, where standard-addition experiments give average recoveries of about 100.5% for Cu2⁺ (RSD 1.63%) and 101.3% for Pb2⁺ (RSD 0.55%), indicating accurate quantification without significant matrix effects. The use of low-cost agricultural waste as a precursor, a simple thermal/chemical activation route, and ethanol/Nafion drop-casting onto commercial GCEs highlights attractive fabrication scalability and low materials cost, yet the study does not report explicit fouling resistance tests, long-term operational stability, batch-to-batch reproducibility, or a systematic cost and field-deployability analysis, which remain open for future development. Another study utilized nano black carbon originating from sweetgum fruit for the detection of Pb(II), Cd(II), Hg(II), and Cu(II) via ASV. The electrode afforded LOD of 1.60, 2.37, 0.34, and 0.25 µg/L for Pb(II), Cd(II), Hg(II) and Cu(II), respectively, while maintaining resistance to interference from Cl–, Al(III), Mn(II), Zn(II), Ca, Co(II), Mg, and SO42–; its applicability was further demonstrated in spiked real water samples186.

An electrochemical Pb(II) sensor was fabricated using a carbon QDs/ZIF-8 composite, wherein CQDs with an average size of 2.47 ± 0.52 nm were synthesized from Platanus acerifoli leaves via carbonization coupled with hydrothermal processing187. Under optimized conditions, the CQDs@ZIF-8-modified electrode exhibits a linear range of 0.207–207.2 µg/L for Pb2⁺ with an LOD of 00.008 µg/L in single-ion solutions, while in mixed systems it still affords nanomolar-level Pb(II) detection (LOD 0.004–0.014 µg/L) with well-resolved stripping peaks in the presence of Cd(II) and/or Cu(II), confirming excellent selectivity and simultaneous multi-metal sensing capability. Structural and surface analyses (BET, XPS, FTIR) show that the composite offers a large specific surface area, abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, and a rich pore network, which promote heavy-metal adsorption and fast charge transfer, explaining the enhanced voltammetric response compared with CQDs or ZIF-8 alone. The sensor maintains its Pb(II) response in the presence of common background ions such as Ca(II), Mg(II), and K at fivefold higher concentration (RSD ≤ 2.68%), and demonstrates good reproducibility and short-term stability, with five independently fabricated CQDs@ZIF-8 electrodes giving closely overlapping ASV responses and an overall RSD of 7.79% after repeated measurements. Another Carbon QDs derived from Celosia cristata flower, then coupled to 2-aminothiazole, used as an electrochemical sensor for Hg(II) and Pb(II) via ASV in various spiked samples, such as river, lake, and tap water samples. Nitrogen- and oxygen-rich functional groups improve sensor sensitivity by facilitating electrostatic interaction-mediated capture of heavy metal ions188. A spent coffee modified CPE enabled ASV detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II), and systematic variation of the modifier (spent coffee) content revealed 50% as the optimum, yielding LODs of 1.86 × 10⁴ and 1.00 × 10⁴ µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water, respectively189. In a related sustainable design, an electrochemical voltametric sensor was fabricated from industrial and electronic waste, where arc furnace dust is repurposed into ferrite nanoparticles and spent battery graphite is converted into nitrogen-doped rGO to form an Arc-ferrite/N-rGO nanocomposite. The CPE modified with this nanocomposite exhibits approximately a 3.5-fold enhancement in faradaic current and accelerated charge-transfer kinetics, supporting simultaneous DPV sensing of Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) with detection limits near 1 µg/L using the 3σ method in simulated pure water, with excellent linear calibration between 10.0 and 150.0 µg/L and high selectivity against frequently encountered interfering ions under optimized conditions190. The sensor was verified using actual industrial wastewater and benchmarked against ICP/OES, positioning the sensor as an implementation of circular-economy principles, enabling the conversion of hazardous waste into functional analytical platforms for future heavy-metal monitoring. Furthermore, Ca-MOF incorporating terephthalic acid recovered from plastic waste served as an ASV sensor for Pb(II), affording linearity from 0.1-10,000 µg/L and negligible interference from Cd(II) and Cr(III), yet a relatively high LOD (3σ method) of 1300 µg/L191.

The analytical performance of methods designed for heavy metal removal is rigorously evaluated through key figures of merit, including LOD, linearity, precision, and recovery. These parameters are essential for validating that a treatment process or monitoring technique is fit for its intended purpose, particularly when dealing with trace concentrations in complex matrices like wastewater.

The evaluation of analytical methods for heavy metal determination in wastewater primarily relies on three principal figures of merit: sensitivity, selectivity, and LOD. These indicators are essential for establishing the suitability of a given technique, especially considering stringent water quality regulations that frequently require detection at the \(\mu \text{g}/\text{L}\) concentration range192. Sensitivity, defined as the change in the recorded signal corresponding to a unit change in analyte concentration, shows considerable variation between different detection approaches. As an illustration, electrochemical biosensors may achieve markedly high sensitivity, such as 0.432 mA/mg/L, for a PANI/ZnO/Urease-modified sensor applied to Hg(II) detection193. In contrast, optical techniques typically express sensitivity as a spectral shift. A recently developed microfiber sensor array incorporating an ion-imprinted polymer demonstrated a high sensitivity of 0.01086 nm/nM toward Cr(III), exploiting the evanescent field surrounding the tapered fiber to intensify the analyte-light interaction194.

Selectivity, or the ability to detect a target ion in the presence of interfering species, remains one of the greatest challenges in heavy metal analysis due to the complex matrices of real wastewater samples. Researchers have employed various strategies to achieve this. Ion-imprinted polymers offer a powerful solution by creating specific recognition sites for a target ion; a sensor array utilizing this technology was able to distinguish Cr(III) and Ni(II) even when interfering ions were present at a concentration of 1 μM194. LOD is arguably the most important metric for confirming that a method meets regulatory standards, which for many toxic metals are set at or below the μg/L level. The pursuit of lower LODs has led to remarkable innovations. For example, the GDMP-LIBS method achieved LODs of 0.2 μg/L for Cu and 0.6 μg/L for Cr, which are orders of magnitude lower than standard methods and well below the minimum allowable concentrations in water quality standards192. Table 1 reports WHO guideline levels for toxic metals such as Pb(II) (10 µg/L), Cd(II) (3 µg/L), and Hg(II) (6 µg/L). Many of the sensors summarized in Table 2 exhibit LODs below these levels, demonstrating their suitability for regulatory-compliant monitoring.

Furthermore, voltammetric methods facilitate intrinsic access to oxidation-state speciation by resolving the distinct redox signals of individual chemical forms under controlled electrochemical conditions. In particular, differential formal potentials, coupled with variations in deposition kinetics at the electrode surface, can be translated into distinct, species-diagnostic responses, thereby allowing concurrent speciation of Cr(III)/Cr(VI), As(III)/As(V), and Hg⁰/Hg(II) within a single analytical protocol. As a representative case, adsorptive stripping voltammetry yields resolved responses for Cr(III) and Cr(VI) in water samples through modulation of the deposition potential or incorporation of oxidation state selective ligands42, and likewise tailoring electrode/electrolyte conditions isolates As(III) from As(V) signals in freshwater samples195. In like manner, mercury speciation can be achieved electrochemically, whereby dissolved Hg(II) is cathodically reduced to Hg⁰ at the electrode and subsequently oxidatively stripped during the voltammetric scan, whereas elemental Hg⁰ (such as gaseous Hg) can be collected on Au-based substrates and then reoxidized to Hg(II) for measurement196,197. The capacity for direct speciation is a defining strength of voltammetric approaches, by comparison, AAS and ICP-MS, which are limited to total metal determinations without valence information and thus require prior fractionation or coupling to chromatographic systems to distinguish different oxidation states30,42. Overall, the intrinsic electrochemical speciation capability (distinguishing Cr(III)/Cr(VI), As(III)/As(V), Hg⁰/Hg(II)) is a major advantage of voltammetry over ICP-MS/AAS, enabling direct multi-species analysis with minimal sample preparation.

The validation of voltammetric sensors using real environmental water samples represents a pivotal intermediate stage in advancing these devices from controlled laboratory studies to practical, in situ monitoring tools. Increasingly, researchers seek to evaluate sensor performance in a range of matrices, such as tap water, river water, and industrial effluents, to demonstrate robustness under complex interferences and fluctuating sample conditions. This validation not only verifies key analytical figures of merit, including sensitivity and limits of detection, but also examines operational reliability in the presence of natural organic matter, pH variability, and competing metal ions that typify genuine environmental systems198,199. Extensive studies have employed tap water matrices for validation, primarily addressing issues of drinking water quality and regulatory conformity. One notable case involves a hand-held voltammetric device, the so-called E-Tongue, which is based on SPCEs modified with gold nano stars and was deployed for community-level assessment of Pb(II) contamination in tap water across several towns in Massachusetts. The instrument showed strong practical performance by successfully analyzing 634 household samples, attaining Pb(II) recoveries between 84% and 105% even in copper-rich conditions, with results in good agreement with established AAS and ICP-MS methods198.

Similarly, additional studies have verified sensor applicability in tap water, for instance, through an Al₂NiCoO₅ nanoflake-modified electrode capable of detecting several heavy metals concurrently and a conductive polymer sensor modified with bismuth nanoparticles for trace metal assessment; in both cases, the reported recovery percentages were satisfactory, confirming the accuracy of these methods in this widely encountered matrix200,201. The analysis of river water is particularly challenging, as this matrix contains suspended particles, natural organic constituents, and a wide variety of potential interfering species. To address these complexities, voltammetric sensors have undergone comprehensive testing in such environments to demonstrate their feasibility for field use. One notable example is a sophisticated electrochemical platform integrating an oxygen-eliminating electrocatalyst that was validated with samples from Tunisia's Mejerda River for the detection of Hg(II), Pb(II), and As(III), enabling precise on-site analysis without the necessity of purging the samples202.

Industrial wastewater represents the most demanding test for any analytical sensor due to its highly complex and often polluted nature, containing high levels of organic and inorganic interferences. Successful validation in this matrix is a testament to a sensor's selectivity and resilience. For example, a sensor based on AuNPs/CPE was applied for the detection of Congo red dye in wastewater samples, with calculated recoveries falling within an acceptable range of 88% to 106%203. The conclusive proof of a voltammetric sensor's analytical competence is demonstrated through its real-sample applications204. This ensures the demonstration of accurate and stable operation within heterogeneous aqueous matrices such as household water, rivers, and industrial effluents, contexts markedly more complex than laboratory solutions205. In such validations, the standard addition method remains the benchmark for addressing unpredictable matrix interferences204. Numerous recent studies corroborate the efficiency of portable voltammetric technologies for rapid, field-level monitoring, yielding data consistent with ICP-MS reference outcomes206. Conducting measurements directly on-site affords key benefits, including minimized contamination and maintaining the native chemical state of analytes207.

Recent progress in nanomaterial-based interfacial engineering, in parallel with the refinement and diversification of voltametric protocols, has markedly improved the sensitivity and selectivity of heavy-metal monitoring. Despite these advances, the upscaling of these electrochemical sensors from proof-of-concept in reliable, field-operable analytical instruments, remains obstructed by a series of systemic bottlenecks. Beyond technical considerations, these issues are rooted in the fundamental separation between idealized experimental conditions, and the chemically heterogeneous nature of real-world samples, which weakens analytical precision and long-term robustness of the sensing platforms208. The lack of harmonized procedures for electrode modification and systematic performance assessment across research groups represents a key constraint, making cross-study benchmarking difficult and obstructing scalable deployment of these systems. Although numerous studies report very low LODs and broad linear ranges, the batch-to-batch reproducibility of modified electrodes, especially for manually drop-cast or complex functionalization schemes, remains problematic for mass production209. In addition, long-term sensor performance under dynamic conditions is frequently not rigorously evaluated, with many reported platforms exhibiting rapid deterioration of the analytical signal caused by mechanical degradation of the modifier film or continuous leaching of active constituents during repeated stripping cycles46. The intellectual emphasis in the field is moving from a “performance-centric” approach to a “reliability-centric” evaluation, highlighting the need for in-situ and operando probes of the evolving electrode/electrolyte interfacial processes. Such reorientation is indispensable for probing the pathways of surface fouling and the interfacial dynamics that shape electron-transfer kinetics in challenging analytical matrices. Moreover, the ecological footprint and toxicological implications associated with large-scale production and disposal of nanomaterial-functionalized electrodes highlight the use of circular material frameworks and green chemistry-based synthesis to secure sustainable operation of the technology210. Mitigating these multi-faceted challenges is a prerequisite for advancing autonomously, real-time monitoring platforms that function reliably in heterogeneous environmental and industrial scenarios.

Operational stability of the modified electrode interfaces remains a central obstacle for implementing continuous and autonomous monitoring. The time-dependent degradation of sensing performance is associated with both the inherent instability of the modifier layer and the progressive contamination of the electrode surface by matrix constituents. A systematic understanding of these intertwined degradation pathways is essential for translating laboratory-demonstrated sensitivity into robust field-deployable platforms.

Regarding the physical integrity of the modifier itself, mechanical stability under hydrodynamic regimes presents a primary challenge, notably during the high-speed agitation commonly employed in stripping voltammetric preconcentration. Furthermore, repeated oxidation–reduction cycles can promote leaching of the active layer, a phenomenon most evident in bismuth and other mercury-free film electrodes, manifesting as a gradual reduction in sensitivity and a systematic shift of peak potentials46. The performance of modified electrodes is also markedly sensitive to environmental fluctuations such as pH and ionic strength, which can compromise both the integrity of the restored sp2 network in carbon-based nanocomposites and the coordination architectures of MOFs, motivating the development of more durable anchoring and coating strategies210.

Electrode fouling, the nonspecific adsorption of matrix species, persists as the principal limitation in electrochemical measurements performed on complex environmental and biological systems. The mechanisms by which fouling agents passivate the electrode surface can be systematically categorized. Organic fouling arises from the accumulation of dissolved organic matter, including humic and fulvic acids, proteins, and anthropogenic surfactants211,212. These macromolecules adsorb onto the electrode, physically masking active sites and impeding the diffusion of target metal ions toward the interface. The resulting passivation reduces the faradaic stripping current and simultaneously generates pronounced overpotentials, complicating peak separation in multi-analyte voltammetry. Inorganic fouling involves the precipitation of metal hydroxides or salts, particularly in hardwater samples, forming insulating surface layers that block electron transfer211. Biofouling, the formation of microbial biofilms, represents a severe challenge in long-term in-situ deployments, as the biofilm creates a diffusive barrier with its own local chemical microenvironment, altering both mass transport and the effective redox conditions at the electrode surface213. A systematic study of fouling in seawater demonstrated that surfactants such as sodium dodecyl sulfate, Triton X-100, and dodecylamine cause a concentration-dependent decline in peak height during DPV measurements of Pb(II), Cd(II), and Cu(II), with the magnitude of signal suppression directly correlated with the surfactant's critical micelle concentration212. Similarly, the accumulation of reaction products generated during high-overpotential processes, such as the oxidation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, represents an additional pathway for electrode passivation214.

The development of effective antifoul interfaces has consequently become a research priority, with strategies broadly classified by their mechanism of protection. Physical barrier approaches utilize size and charge-selective coatings that block macromolecular foulants while permitting the permeation of small metal ions. Silica isoporous membranes, formed by the self-assembly of surfactant-templated mesoporous silica, have been integrated with SPEs to exclude proteins and humic substances based on size exclusion, while maintaining sensitivity for Pb(II) and Cd(II) detection215. Similarly, hydrogel coatings based on cross-linked hydrophilic polymers create a hydrated interfacial layer that resists protein adsorption through entropic and osmotic effects216. Chemical antifouling strategies involve the design of surfaces that actively repel nonspecific adsorption. Zwitterionic polymer films, containing equimolar positive and negative charges, form a tightly bound hydration layer that renders the surface thermodynamically unfavorable for protein and organic matter adhesion. These zwitterionic interfaces have shown promise for maintaining voltammetric performance in biological fluids and wastewater matrices217. A third category, sacrificial or self-cleaning architecture, employs materials that degrade fouling agents or regenerate the active surface under electrochemical control, though this approach adds complexity to the measurement protocol218.

A notable illustration of the efficacy of antifouling design is the comparative evaluation of Bi₂WO₆ coupled with rGO vs a g-C3N4 composite. The former experienced a severe reduction in average current density in both human serum and human plasma, highlighting the impact of biofouling on unprotected electrode surfaces. By contrast, the incorporation of g-C3N4 composite yielded a sensor with robust antifouling performance, enabling the determination of Zn, Hg, Pb, and Cd in wastewater at LODs of 0.045, 0.030, 0.019, and 0.09 μg/L, respectively, calculated using the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water and cross-validated by ICP-MS46. Despite these advances, the long-term operational performance of antifouling coatings under harsh and dynamically fluctuating environment including extremes of temperature, ionic strength, and biological activity, remains insufficiently substantiated, and systematic comparative studies across multiple real-world matrices are notably absent from the current literature.

Compounding the challenge of fouling, the complexity of calibration procedures and the intrinsic drift of the voltammetric signal further limit the practical applicability of voltammetric sensing platforms. The sensor output exhibits pronounced sensitivity to environmental variables such as temperature, pH, and ionic strength, which can cause the response to diverge considerably from the original calibration model210. For complex real-world samples, matrix-induced biases typically necessitate the use of standard addition methods, a workflow that is time-demanding and challenging to integrate into automated field measurements212. Consequently, chemometric approaches combined with ML algorithms are being increasingly adopted to manage baseline drift, deconvolute complex multi-analyte signatures, and strengthen predictive modeling in dynamically fluctuating matrices219.

While the limitations of the standard addition method are acknowledged, the field has witnessed rapid advancements in data-driven calibration that extend well beyond traditional chemometric approaches. Recent years have seen a paradigm shift toward ML-assisted calibration strategies that address the fundamental constraints of conventional methods, particularly their inability to correct for non-matrix interferences such as intermetallic compound formation, which standard addition cannot resolve53.

Advanced machine-learning frameworks, including support vector regression, random forest, XGBoost, and artificial neural networks, are now being integrated with ASV to surmount its limitations. For example, coupling SVR with square-wave ASV enabled precise concurrent detection of Cd (II), Pb (II), Cu (II), and Hg (II); SVR attained area-under-ROC values exceeding 0.95, and XGBoost concentration-prediction models achieved R2 values above 0.95 in complex multi-ion samples220. Similarly, radial basis function artificial neural networks (RBF-ANN) have been successfully applied to resolve strongly overlapping voltammetric peaks of lead and tin at bismuth film electrodes, outperforming conventional chemometric methods, including principal component regression (PCR), partial least squares (PLS), and least-squares support vector machines (LS-SVM)36. Beyond peak deconvolution, ML algorithms are increasingly deployed to compensate for signal drift, correct for electrode fouling, and enable adaptive recalibration under field conditions-capabilities that are essential for autonomous, long-term environmental monitoring221. ML and chemometric approaches have been comprehensively reviewed as powerful tools for signal deconvolution, drift compensation, and adaptive calibration, with particular emphasis on their incorporation into continuous monitoring workflows221. These data-driven calibration strategies not only enhance analytical accuracy in complex matrices but also reduce the frequency of resource-intensive recalibration, supporting the feasibility of decentralized, real-time heavy metal surveillance53. Nevertheless, compromises persist: the superior predictive performance of black-box ML models must be weighed against the mechanistic interpretability needed for regulatory approval, and ensuring transferability across varied environmental matrices and sensor platforms remains a significant unresolved issue.

The exceptional selectivity and sensitivity of voltammetric sensors are often evaluated in compositionally well-defined buffer solutions. However, extending this performance to practical samples is frequently hindered by the sample matrix. Untreated environmental samples can degrade electrode performance via electrode fouling, and lead to severe signal suppression. In practice, sample pretreatment can be more than a simple preparatory task, functioning as a critical operation that determines the trustworthiness of quantification. Field-deployable implementations can depend critically on their successful integration.

Raw environmental waters pose multiple challenges, notably the strong binding of heavy metal ions to humic and fulvic acids, which produce electro-inactive species that are not captured by the sensor response222. Additionally, the sensitivity of voltammetric detection is strongly pH dependent. Since field samples span a wide pH range, achieving optimal detection demands stringent pH control, a requirement that is difficult under on-site conditions where liquid reagents must be manipulated223. Moreover, suspended solids can physically damage the electrode surface, whereas microbial activity can promote biofouling.

In response, the field is moving away from elaborate, confined methods toward small-footprint, automated, reagent-free approaches designed for in situ use. A key approach involves traditional solid-phase extraction that selectively captures heavy metals, effectively separating and pre-concentrating the analyte from the sample matrix224,225. Moreover, instead of relying on added buffers, pH can be modulated electrochemically. An anodically polarized acidifier electrode catalyzes water electrolysis, generating protons at the sensor interface and precisely adjusting the local pH223,226. Adsorptive matrix removal with Amberlite is a fast sample clean-up step where the sample is mixed with the resin to scavenge organic surfactants and humic substances before analysis227. One of the ideal solutions is integrated lab-on-a-chip (LOC) platforms, which allow sample pretreatment to be performed on the same platform as detection. A foldable, paper-based microfluidic design brings together sample collection, pretreatment, and standard addition, automating traditional laboratory workflows in a compact format suitable for on-site heavy metal analysis228,229. Every pretreatment approach designed for field use involves an inherent trade-off. Simple filtration and acidification are appealing from a practical standpoint, yet may not achieve complete liberation of metals or adequate suppression of interferences. The next frontier lies in integrating these pretreatment capabilities directly with electrochemical sensor platforms, enabling a shift from basic measurements in relatively clear waters to resilient, automated monitoring in turbid and organic-rich environments.

Advancing voltammetric sensors from proof-of-concept laboratory prototypes to regulatory-ready analytical instruments is critically dependent on achieving high inter-electrode reproducibility and durable long-term operational stability. Although nanomaterial-enhanced sensing platforms typically offer remarkable sensitivity, the intrinsic physicochemical complexity of these materials, including batch-to-batch variations in particle size, defect density, and surface chemistry, poses significant challenges to process standardization230. Manual drop-casting often yields coatings with uneven thickness and non-uniform distribution, resulting in significant sensor-to-sensor differences in electroactive surface area (EASA) and electron-transfer kinetics, highlighting the necessity for precise, scalable fabrication technologies such as inkjet printing, screen-printing, or controlled electrodeposition to ensure adequate analytical reliability for large-scale environmental monitoring231. For instance, the reproducibility assessment of ZIF-8/CNT using five independently prepared electrodes for the detection of Pb(II) yielded relative standard deviations of 8.51%232.

A major remaining challenge is operational stability, notably when sensors are exposed to hydrodynamic stress and repeated potential scans typical of stripping voltammetry. A 30-day monitoring of the ZIF-8/CNT electrode stability revealed that the oxidation peak currents of Cd(II) and Pb(II) followed a decreasing trend, pointing to a gradual deterioration in stability232. Moreover, the progressive decline in current sensitivity under repeated measurements limits the practical applicability of the voltammetric sensor. As a representative case, detection of Cu(II) at 2.54 × 10−11 µg/L gave five current responses of 0.059, 0.122, 0.059, 0.121, and 0.097 mA, corresponding to an RSD of 34%233.

The broad commercialization and on-site implementation of voltammetric sensors for heavy-metal monitoring remain constrained by the intersection of economic feasibility, scalable manufacturing, and the lack of standardized regulatory provisions. Although laboratory-scale prototypes often exhibit outstanding analytical responses, their translation to high-volume manufacturing frequently stalls in the “Valley of Death”, primarily because the use of expensive nanostructured materials MOFs, COFs, and noble metal nanoparticles such as Au, Pt, and Ag, significantly inflates the cost per analysis234. In response to these cost-related barriers, emerging studies prioritize the use of sustainable, low-cost systems such as biomass-derived carbons and SPCEs, which are well established to be low-cost, facile, and highly reproducible in fabrication, making them ideal for large-scale production; mass production has already made SPCEs inexpensive and maintenance free235. Further, flexible and fully printed low-cost graphene sensors fabricated via additive manufacturing are being investigated as sustainable and scalable alternatives for heavy metal detection236; inkjet-printed sensors in particular offer a mass-automated printing process, uniform deposition, minimal material waste, and scalable manufacturing236. One of the most promising techniques for truly industrial-scale fabrication is roll-to-roll gravure printing, which can produce uniform redox kinetics on flexible substrate rolls of up to 150 m length, enabling large-scale, low-cost production of disposable wearable sensors for heavy metal monitoring237.

A formidable challenge, especially during the transition from manual laboratory procedures to automated, industrial-scale processes, where uniformity in nanomaterial attributes, film thickness, and EASA across large batches is required to maintain electrode-to-electrode consistency and analytical reliability208. To this end, advanced manufacturing approaches such as roll-to-roll printing, inkjet printing, and controlled electrodeposition are under active exploration to realize large-scale manufacturing of uniform sensing interfaces. Notably, inkjet printing yields thin, laterally uniform coatings and significantly reduced electrode-to-electrode variability compared to conventional drop-casting, which tends to produce edge-enriched films with pronounced interfacial heterogeneity. Electrochemical performance is governed by the spatial accessibility and distribution of the functional layer rather than by the nominal deposited mass, which positions the deposition technique among the primary design parameters in sensor development238. Nevertheless, integrating these electrochemical platforms within established IoT architectures and microfluidic networks for widespread environmental monitoring calls for additional optimization of energy consumption, data-transfer reliability, and long-term robustness239.

Building on recent demonstrations of low-cost transducer platforms, disposable electrochemical paper-based devices can be fabricated for less than 0.20 USD per unit using commodity carbons such as graphite powder combined with simple binders on cellulose substrates, without compromising voltammetric performance240. Paper-based electrochemical chips can be prepared for less than USD1.00 per device, with some designs costing as little as USD0.05241,242,243. These screen-printed paper analytical devices avoid expensive noble-metal inks and sophisticated readout hardware, echoing the broader trend toward minimalist, point-of-care architectures highlighted for MIP-based systems.

Similarly, integrating MIPs as synthetic recognition elements on reusable carbon or GCEs can be produced at a total cost of less than USD2.10 per test (including all consumables), while the same glassy carbon electrode can be reused for up to 100 measurements, further lowering the per-test cost when the transducer is cycled244. Even sub-cent sensor costs between 0.01 and 0.09 USD have been reported for paper-based MIP platforms that still operate at femtomolar-level limits of detection, underscoring how mass production and simplified imprinting can drive prices down without sacrificing sensitivity245. These ultra-low-cost designs are particularly attractive for heavy metal sensing in resource-limited or field settings, where single-use formats, ease of storage, and minimal sample preparation are essential. Continued development of low-cost conductive inks, recyclable substrates, and scalable MIP integration strategies (e.g., roll-to-roll imprinting or in situ electropolymerization) is therefore pivotal to translating electrochemical HM sensors from laboratory prototypes into commercially and regulatorily viable environmental monitoring tools246.

From a regulatory standpoint, the adoption of voltammetric methods is hindered by the gap between advanced sensor technologies and established environmental standards. Current frameworks, such as those mandated by the EU DWD, and US EPA, still exclusively recognize ‘gold standard' laboratory protocols like ICP and AAS247. The recast EU Drinking Water Directive has introduced new lower thresholds for heavy metal contamination in drinking water (e.g., Pb reduced from 10 µg/L to 5 µg/L by 2036, Cr from 50 µg/L to 25 µg/L), creating an urgent impetus for sensitive in‑situ electrochemical sensors that can meet these stricter limits248. The current lack of harmonized protocols governing calibration, validation, and reporting, regulatory acceptance of electrochemical sensing remains a major barrier. Establishing regulatory readiness will require broad cross-validation efforts with benchmark methods in real-sample matrices, coupled with the introduction of enforceable criteria for sensor performance and reliability249.

Commercialization demands a staged (technology readiness level) TRL roadmap: bench-top drop-cast electrodes are re-engineered into screen-printed carbon inks on flexible substrates at TRL 4-5, followed by roll-to-roll gravure production at TRL 6-7 to drive unit costs below USD 0.20 and guarantee batch-to-batch electrochemical uniformity. Initial market entry should target early-adopter sectors, such as mining remediation, industrial discharge monitoring, and resource-limited settings where rapid on-site turnaround outweighs laboratory precision. Finally, integrating the printed sensor with a miniaturized potentiostat, low-power microcontroller, and telemetry module will yield autonomous field-deployable nodes, closing the gap between laboratory prototypes and widespread IoT-enabled environmental monitoring networks205,250,251,252.

In summary, despite substantial progress, several critical knowledge gaps continue to hinder the translation of advanced voltammetric sensors into robust field technologies. No universal anti-fouling strategy has yet demonstrated stable, weeks-long performance in untreated natural waters with high organic loads, and protocols for calibration, interference testing, and long-term stability assessment remain poorly harmonized across laboratories, limiting comparability and regulatory acceptance. ML models trained on specific sensor batches or local water matrices frequently fail to generalize to new sites or electrode platforms, underscoring the need for large, open-access voltammetric datasets and domain-adaptive algorithms. In parallel, the synthesis of high-performance materials such as MOFs and COFs is often too complex and costly for large-scale manufacturing. Addressing these challenges will require coordinated, interdisciplinary efforts focused on developing scalable, low-toxicity anti-fouling strategies validated in long-term field trials, establishing community-adopted validation protocols, creating rich open datasets coupled with transferable ML models, and simplifying synthetic routes to enable cost-effective production of next-generation sensing materials.

The convergence of advanced electrode materials with microfluidic platforms and ML algorithms represents a paradigm shift in environmental monitoring, enabling the transition from discrete laboratory measurements to continuous, autonomous, and in-situ analysis. Microfluidic devices, often termed LOC systems, provide tight control over very small fluid quantities, supporting automated sample handling, reagent mixing, and embedding of sensors in a compact device, which markedly cuts sample and reagent requirements, decreases contamination likelihood, and enhances analytical throughput253.

With accelerating industrial development, the detection and removal of heavy metals from wastewater and various water resources have become a pressing necessity, given the substantial risks they pose to human health and aquatic environments. Despite offering excellent sensitivity and precision, traditional laboratory techniques such as ICP-MS and AAS are not ideal for large-scale, on-site monitoring, primarily due to their high expense, operational complexity, and the requirement for sample collection, transportation, and prior pretreatment52,254. Because of these restrictions, there has been growing interest in portable and on-site voltammetric platforms, which combine device miniaturization, low-cost operation, and accelerated analysis with the capacity to conduct real-time or near-real-time surveillance using minimally processed samples210,254. By incorporating techniques like ASV and SWV, these devices deliver the high analytical sensitivity demanded for trace metal quantification, making them ideal candidates for decentralized environmental monitoring applications52,210.

In recent years, work in this field has largely targeted the enhancement of sensor capabilities by integrating advanced nanostructured materials and streamlined electrochemical platforms to resolve challenges such as interference from dissolved oxygen and reliance on time-consuming pretreatment procedures. An illustrative case is an “all-in-one” electrochemical sensor based on a dual SPCE system. Here, one electrode is engineered with an electrocatalytic oxygen-filtering layer made of Au nanofoam combined with Pt nanoparticles, which removes dissolved oxygen in the vicinity of the electrode and therefore obviates the sample purging step. The second working electrode, covered with polypyrrole, Au nanofoam, and WS₂ nanosheets, facilitates simultaneous electrochemical detection of Hg(II), Pb(II), and As(III). The system provided LODs that were below the US EPA guidelines for drinking water, indicating its effectiveness for reliable field analysis in challenging matrices, including river water202.

In an alternative strategy for streamlining on-site analyses, investigators introduced a local electrochemical acidification technique for the sample solution. This approach employs a platinum grid acidifier electrode that electrolyzes water to generate protons directly inside the measurement cell, thereby eliminating the requirement for external reagents for pH adjustment. Applied to Pb(II) determination using a gold screen-printed electrode, the system provided a detection limit of 6 μg/L calculated based on the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water, below the WHO guideline value for lead in drinking water, and exhibited a linear response in the range of 10–100 μg/L223. The technological evolution of these analytical platforms has also resulted in fully automated stand-alone systems tailored for extended or remote operation. An illustrative example is a portable flow system based on ASV, incorporating flow cells, a potentiostat operated via remote control, and automated software that supervises the entire measurement sequence, including sensor cleaning and subsequent regeneration. This approach has been applied successfully to the simultaneous analysis of Cd (II), Pb (II), and Cu (II) in river water, with detection limits in the sub-parts per billion (ppb) domains, underscoring its effectiveness for continuous, online monitoring of natural water bodies254. Further expanding the range of detectable metals, a solid bismuth microelectrode was utilized for the on-site determination of Se (IV) ions. By applying differential pulse CSV, this eco-friendly, miniaturized sensor achieved an exceptionally low detection limit 0.055 μg/L calculated based on the 3σ method in simulated ultrapure water with a short accumulation period, while exhibiting high accuracy and strong anti-interference performance in environmental water samples using the standard addition method255. Likewise, a portable sensing platform based on a disposable screen-printed electrode bearing an in-situ deposited bismuth film was constructed for the determination of Cd(II) and Pb(II). The approach demonstrated excellent analytical performance, providing linear calibration ranges of 20–80 μg/L for Cd(II) and 10–80 μg/L for Pb(II), with LOD of 1.20 μg/L and 0.96 μg/L, respectively256. Figure 8 reveals a schematic illustration of on-site electrochemical detection of heavy metals in industrial wastewater using portable SPEs and a smartphone-integrated potentiostat.

Conceptual illustration of an intelligent sensing framework for heavy metal monitoring in surface waters using SPEs coupled to an IoT cloud platform and ML. Industrial effluents are discharged into the aquatic environment, where a portable SPE device operated by a field analyst acquires voltammetric signals and wirelessly transmits them to the IoT cloud platform. ML algorithms perform calibration, sensor-drift correction, and multi-metal quantification (e.g., Pb, Cd, and Cu), converting raw electrochemical data into concentration- and risk-level outputs based on WHO drinking-water guidelines. The dashboard panels summarize current risk status, visualize historical trends, and support closed-loop feedback actions such as sending alerts, initiating mitigation, and generating reports for decision-makers.
